0:00 I've been marketing for 13 years I built

0:11 money piece of advice number one start

0:19 start there then you can go up it's very
0:21 difficult to come down and in the

0:26 the system and so it's totally fine to
0:28 start it free and then start bumping up

0:31 quick examples and then I'll show you

0:59 think it was $500 or $1,000 was their

11:23 the return on effort for better exceeds
11:26 one incremental unit of more meaning if
11:29 I've got one sales guy if I add a second
11:32 sales guy then I think I have a high
11:33 degree of certainty that I can probably
11:35 double my sales by doing that but when

11:59 money per unit of effort and so in the

12:03 as much volume through there as possible
12:04 that's the create flow monetize flow
12:06 then net friction the the optimization
12:08 steps really only matter when you have
12:10 flow going through and so you do more

12:16 but once you then go through the better

12:28 orders of magnitude and when you have so

13:26 constrain that and then grow into the

1:03:25 is the positive and so we want to

13 Years of Marketing Advice in 85 Mins

0:00 I've been marketing for 13 years I built
0:01 and sold nine companies I sold my last
0:03 company for $ 46.2 million to American
0:05 Pacific group I've also written two
0:06 books on marketing that have sold over a
0:08 million copies here's 13 years of
0:10 marketing advice that will make you more
0:11 money piece of advice number one start
0:13 with low prices or even gasp free the
0:17 reason that I say that is because if you
0:19 start there then you can go up it's very
0:21 difficult to come down and in the
0:23 beginning you want to get flow through

27:07 LT GP so I use ltp to be more specific
27:10 lifetime value is just the amount of
27:11 gross profit you make from a customer
27:13 over the lifetime of them being a
27:15 customer of your business CAC is the
27:17 cost to require a customer which is the
27:19 all-in cost including sales marketing
27:21 software everything to get someone to
27:23 give you money and so the cact TV ratio
27:25 is simply how much money it costs you to
27:27 make more money it's the foundational
27:29 economic unit of any business and so
27:32 making sure that you know how much money
27:33 it costs you to make money that
27:34 fundamentally feels like a very
27:36 important metric in order to scale the
27:37 business and over time as long as you
27:39 don't have uh some big Capital expenses
27:41 in terms of scaling your business that
27:44 fundamental return that you get will
27:46 reflect the compounding growth of the
27:47 business provided you don't have other
27:48 constraints to growth so for example if
27:51 I have meals that I sell for $10 and it
27:54 cost me $9 to package and ship them and
27:57 deliver for them then I have $1 of gross
28:00 profit per meal and so if someone buys
28:03 10 meals for me I'm not making $100 I'm
28:05 making $10 in gross profit and if the
28:07 average person buys call it five weeks
28:10 of meals then I would make $50 in
28:12 lifetime gross profit and so the only
28:14 way that I would want to be able to
28:15 acquire customers is that the cost to
28:17 acquire customer in this example I'd
28:18 want to have probably at $15 or less
28:21 because you can't do it at even because
28:22 then there's no money left over to do
28:23 everything else in the business and so
28:25 the rule of thumb is that you want have
28:26 at least 3 to one on LTV to CAC but
28:29 again LTV is not lifetime Revenue it's
28:31 lifetime gross profit right and that's
28:33 what people I think a lot of times mess
28:34 up is they don't even know what their

17 Years of Marketing Advice in 46 Mins

0:00 So, if there's one thing that I'm good
0:02 at, it is marketing. I have got
0:04 thousands of clients. I've generated
0:06 those clients over 7.8 billion dollars
0:08 in sales. I've gotten these results in
0:10 over 1,067 different niches for clients
0:13 that are in 136 different countries
0:16 around the world. I've got 100 team
0:18 members in my agency, and I'm also an
0:20 investor on Shark Tank. Today, what I'm
0:22 going to be doing is just giving you
0:24 everything that I've learned along my
0:26 journey. All the mistakes that I've paid
0:28 for in full so I can shortcut wherever
0:30 you are on your learning curve
0:33 on becoming a marketer. There is no way
0:36 that you cannot watch this video, and if
0:38 you take notes and actually implement
0:40 the stuff that I'm going to tell you for
0:42 you not to make way more money than what
0:44 you're making right now. So, what is
0:46 marketing? Marketing
0:48 in general, I believe, is probably the
0:50 most important and valuable skill that
0:52 you can ever acquire because, regardless
0:54 of the career that you choose or the
0:56 different path that you walk down in
0:58 your life, you're going to be faced in
1:00 some way in either marketing a business
1:03 or marketing yourself. And it is
1:04 fundamentally a skill that can change
1:07 the trajectory of your life like no
1:09 other. Marketing is the activity of
1:13 promoting and selling products and
1:16 services. But, more importantly than
1:17 that, it's also in not only attracting
1:21 and getting clients but retaining them
1:22 and then being able to deliver on your
1:24 promises. Because all successful
1:26 businesses are focused on not being in
1:28 the order business but being in the
1:30 reorder business. Do you want to be in a
1:32 situation where your clients are worth
1:35 more to you than any other person in
1:38 your industry and your market?
1:40 Therefore, you can spend the most to not
1:42 only acquire those clients, but to also
1:45 service those clients and have team
1:47 members come in and help you service
1:48 them and being able to have the funds to
1:51 actually pay for them. This brings us to
1:53 step number one, product versus
1:54 marketing. There are so many people in
1:56 the business world that sit in one camp
1:58 that it's like it's just all about
1:59 product. If you have a great product,
2:01 then you're going to be able to go out
2:02 there and crush anybody cuz the word's
2:04 going to spread and everyone's going to
2:06 come to you and then they're going to
2:07 want to buy your thing. And then, you've
2:09 got another school of thought that it's
2:11 just like all you need is marketing.
2:13 Like you just have like marketing and
2:15 you can sell anything. I believe that
2:17 both of these people are wrong. It's not
2:19 about product or it's not about
2:21 marketing, but it really is about both.
2:24 Not all businesses are sexy. Just
2:26 focusing only on product and thinking
2:28 that that is the only thing is that you
2:30 need is a lie. And I've seen so many
2:34 businesses die because of this. They set
2:36 up their business, they develop a logo,
2:38 and then they just spend like a year or
2:41 2 years of their life perfecting their
2:43 product or service. They launch their
2:46 business and they never get any
2:48 customers even though their product is
2:49 great. They can never ever scale it
2:52 because they're not marketing it, and
2:53 then it just becomes this job. But, it's
2:55 worse than a job for them because
2:57 they're not getting paid holiday.
2:58 Instead of working 40 hours a week,
3:00 they're working 80 hours a week, and
3:02 their life is just left like the scraps
3:05 of what's left from their business. And
3:07 then, you have the other angle where the
3:08 marketer that goes out there and he has
3:11 no
3:12 emphasis on product and customer
3:14 experience. He's got a shitty product
3:16 that he's pouring gasoline on and it
3:18 spreads and then the negative word of
3:20 mouth spreads and then it's gone. And
3:22 then, they're on to the next business.
3:23 They just keep on launching new offers
3:25 and new businesses and new markets
3:28 because they don't have any emphasis on
3:30 product. And realistically, if you look
3:32 at the biggest businesses in the world,
3:35 those businesses have an exceptional
3:37 product that they market exceptionally
3:40 well. You really need to get out of the
3:43 mindset of it's just product or it's
3:45 just marketing, which brings us to step
3:48 number two, to sell something that the
3:50 market is starving for. When I got
3:53 started in my entrepreneurial career,
3:55 started by selling water filters. And I
3:57 had to learn a very hard lesson.
3:59 Everyone in Australia at the time had
4:02 the perception that it was completely
4:03 fine to drink the tap water. So, I was
4:06 entering into a market that people were
4:08 not starving for the product. And
4:10 instead of having a compelling offer
4:12 that solves a burning problem, I was
4:15 trying to have a convincing argument.
4:17 And everything was like just pushing a
4:20 ball up a hill and swimming against the
4:23 raging current that was the river of the
4:26 desires of my marketplace. When you're
4:28 launching a business, you want to launch
4:30 with a minimal viable product or a
4:33 minimal viable offer. And that is just
4:36 enough for it to be good enough for you
4:39 to sell it. And you want to do that to
4:42 test the appetite that there is in the
4:44 marketplace. And then, only once you
4:47 have proven that there is an appetite
4:49 for what it is that you're selling, do
4:50 you then want to go and develop the back
4:53 end of the business. Most people, if you
4:55 speak to them, they all have a business
4:57 idea, right? Like everyone has a
4:59 business idea and then they're like,
5:00 "Oh, I can't do that because I saw
5:02 somebody else had a business in that
5:04 niche or I've seen that idea before."
5:07 And they spend most of their life
5:09 looking for a completely unique idea for
5:12 the business that it is that they want
5:13 to start. That is a horrible, horrible
5:16 idea. You want to look for markets that
5:19 there are lots of people in that niche,
5:21 and it is competitive because that has
5:24 proven that there is a need. And so, the
5:27 most pivotal thing above everything when
5:29 it comes to marketing is market
5:31 selection. You can have the sexiest
5:33 logo, the best team, the most dialed-in
5:36 funnel. If you are in the wrong market,
5:40 nothing else matters. And when it comes
5:43 to product, the overarching advice that
5:46 I can give anybody is to build a
5:49 business that you would want to be a
5:52 customer of. Third point is direct
5:54 response versus brand. Direct response,
5:57 if you don't know, is the format of
6:00 advertising where you're putting out
6:01 messages in front of a marketplace that
6:04 elicits a direct response. Call this
6:07 number, go to our website, click this
6:10 button, become a lead, or buy something.
6:14 Where you've got the brand world
6:16 advertising to tell people that you
6:18 exist, to reinforce a message,
6:22 or a lifestyle that you are trying to
6:25 depict. If you ask most people like,
6:27 "What is the best brand in the world?"
6:29 Apple is a brand that comes up in that
6:31 conversation a lot. And what you'll find
6:33 is that a lot of these brands, they all
6:36 got their roots in direct response. Like
6:38 Apple, they were cold calling computer
6:41 dealers to get their computers stocked
6:43 into those networks. And a lot of the
6:44 things that they were doing in the
6:45 beginning like were direct selling. It
6:48 was direct response marketing.
6:50 Throughout their evolution, they earned
6:52 the right to become a brand. This is an
6:56 argument that I see a lot online. People
6:59 are talking about, "Hey, like it's all
7:00 about hardcore direct response." Or they
7:03 sit in the brand camp and that is the
7:05 only thing that they think is important.
7:07 The reality of it is, there is big
7:09 businesses that have been created in
7:11 both of those categories. You've got
7:12 businesses like Guthy-Renker. They do 2
7:15 billion dollars a year in sales. They
7:18 are behind Tony Robbins, blew him up
7:21 using infomercials. They've got like
7:23 Proactiv. They've got brands with J.Lo.
7:26 And they've been able to build a
7:27 massive, monstrous business using direct
7:31 response. You've got LVMH group that
7:34 have Louis Vuitton. They've got Sephora.
7:36 They've got a whole range of brands
7:39 under their umbrella. Yet, these guys,
7:41 they spend 16 billion dollars a year on
7:45 advertising. I see so many people, they
7:48 think it's all about brand and they'll
7:49 sit down and they'll come up with these
7:52 beautiful cut up color palettes and the
7:54 typography and the sans serif and the
7:57 logo and the brand mark and the website
8:00 and all of this kind of stuff. They do
8:01 not have a brand, most of these people.
8:04 They have a logo. In order to have a
8:06 brand, you need to have customers. The
8:08 best place to start if you're getting
8:11 out and you do not have huge amounts of
8:13 venture capital is to start in direct
8:16 response. Whether it's at local markets,
8:19 it's cold calling people, it's local
8:21 DMing people, it's you being the
8:23 influencer and creating the organic
8:25 TikTok content in the beginning. Only
8:27 once you do that and you intimately
8:28 understand your customer, you understand
8:30 what's important to your market, will
8:32 you understand how to actually build a
8:34 brand. You're using direct response,
8:36 you're making money, you're spending
8:37 that money to make the product or
8:38 service even better, you're building the
8:40 brand, you're attracting the right
8:42 people that can help you then go and
8:43 build that brand even further, and that
8:45 just accelerates and accelerates and
8:47 accelerates. And then, you can focus on
8:49 more brand building exercises of like,
8:52 "What is important to us as a company?
8:55 What do we stand for? What is the
8:57 problem that we solve for the market?
8:59 Who is the customer and what is
9:01 important to them?" And you can start to
9:03 spend money that emphasizes those things
9:07 within your direct response marketing
9:10 mix, and you can keep that going like a
9:12 flywheel. And so, that is what I have
9:14 seen from all the brands that I have
9:16 worked with, which is all the way from
9:18 small to mom-and-pop businesses all the
9:20 way up to multi-billion dollar
9:23 behemoths. Point number four is organic
9:27 versus paid. Another two very defined
9:31 groups of thought. Some people think,
9:33 "Hey, you always have to be able to
9:35 spend money to acquire a customer." And
9:37 then, you've got one school of thought
9:39 that it's like only focus on putting
9:41 content out and getting customers
9:43 organically and have that word of mouth
9:44 spread. But, both of those are resources
9:47 that you're paying with. One of them is
9:48 you're using money, and the other one
9:50 that you're using time. There is no such
9:52 thing as free advertising. Organic is
9:55 meant to be in a way where it sounds
9:57 like it's free, but it's not. It costs
9:59 money for me to have these cameras and
10:01 videographers and lighting and mic and
10:03 the time and energy that is required in
10:05 order to produce that content as well.
10:08 So, they all come with a cost. When I
10:11 started, you know, I started in direct
10:12 selling, I was cold calling businesses,
10:14 I got a few clients, I got enough to
10:16 start running some direct response style
10:18 ads, and only after I was doing more
10:21 than $10 million a year in revenue did I
10:23 start doing all of this stuff and
10:25 producing YouTube content and doing all
10:27 of those things. I do believe that
10:29 organic shows huge huge potential.
10:32 That's why I'm spending the time and
10:33 energy and doing what it is that I need
10:34 to do, but I would not rely purely on
10:38 organic to build my business. One of the
10:41 things that has become more apparent
10:42 with Instagram Reels and TikTok is that
10:46 you want to use organic to feed the paid
10:50 engine. So, it's a great way now that
10:52 you can get distribution on these
10:54 platforms without having huge follower
10:56 bases for you to test hooks and for you
10:59 to test messaging that works in your
11:02 marketplace and then finding out what
11:04 works organically and then turning that
11:07 into an ad and then amplifying it. The
11:10 customer journey is not like a funnel
11:13 anymore. It's changed. The people that
11:15 are going to maybe discover you from an
11:16 ad, they're going to do a Google sniff
11:18 test, go to your YouTube, watch two
11:20 videos, then get retargeted on Facebook,
11:24 download a high-value content offer or
11:26 some type of opt-in or register for a
11:28 webinar, watch that, go on your email
11:31 list, get five emails, book in a call,
11:33 and buy. One feeds the other. You want
11:36 to have a combination of both. If you
11:38 start with organic and there's no one
11:40 looking at your stuff and that
11:42 frustrates you and you stay there,
11:44 that's ultimately going to kill your
11:46 business. So, it's not either or, it's
11:48 both. Point number five is storytelling.
11:50 Storytelling, above all else when it
11:53 comes to building businesses, brands,
11:56 and going out there and getting lots of
11:57 customers, is
11:59 bar none
12:01 the most highly leveraged skill that you
12:03 can have. And a lot of people talk about
12:05 storytelling, but then they don't really
12:08 tell you like what are the steps that it
12:10 is that you need to take in order to
12:12 become a better storyteller. And that's
12:14 what I'm going to do for you. And the
12:16 best place to start is being a good
12:19 writer
12:20 and being able to clearly articulate
12:23 what it is that you want to say.
12:25 Because you've got a side of your
12:26 business that's going to be the employer
12:28 brand and wanting to attract team
12:31 members into your business, where you're
12:32 going to need to be able to very clearly
12:34 articulate why it would be great to come
12:36 and join your team. And then you've got
12:38 the storytelling component of how do you
12:40 actually tell compelling stories that
12:43 get people to want to buy your things.
12:45 So, the first thing to understand is
12:47 that a good writer is a good thinker.
12:50 They have clear thoughts. Because when
12:52 you sit down to write something, by
12:54 definition, you have to get clear of
12:56 what it is that you want to write,
12:58 whether it's a job ad or it's a Facebook
13:00 ad or it's a landing page copy, you have
13:03 to actually crystallize your thoughts
13:06 and then write them down. You then want
13:08 to think about the clarity of your
13:09 writing.
13:11 And a great app that I use that helps
13:13 with this is the Hemingway app. Now, I
13:15 have studied the New York Times
13:17 best-seller list, the best YouTubers,
13:20 the people that have been elected into
13:22 the most powerful offices in the world,
13:25 and the commonality that you will see in
13:28 all of their forms of storytelling is
13:31 that they write and speak at a
13:34 sixth-grade reading level or below. And
13:37 the reason for that is that you will not
13:40 alienate people
13:42 by having your message too clear to
13:44 consume, but you will certainly alienate
13:46 people by making it so verbose and over
13:50 the top and hard to comprehend that
13:53 people will stop paying attention to
13:55 your message. The next part of
13:56 storytelling is really in your
13:58 communication style. So, communication
14:01 is something that you need to create
14:03 time in your week or in your schedule to
14:06 become better at. Because you're going
14:08 to be communicating until the day that
14:10 you die and you're not only going to be
14:12 getting the leverage in that within your
14:13 business, you'll be getting that in all
14:15 your relationships. Like anything, it's
14:17 a skill that can be acquired and you
14:19 need to practice it. The next thing when
14:20 it comes to storytelling is hooks and
14:23 framing. And a hook, by definition, is
14:26 something that does just that. It hooks
14:28 people.
14:29 It is a statement that you can grab the
14:32 attention of a group of people and make
14:35 them pay attention to what it is that
14:38 you've got to say. One good hook
14:41 is worth more
14:43 than a thousand of the most masterful
14:46 crafted copy that exists. And you want
14:48 to spend a disproportionate amount of
14:51 your time on the hooks. Because if you
14:54 can't hook somebody, it doesn't matter
14:57 how good the rest of that piece of
14:58 communication is. With this, you want to
15:01 be a student of markets, you want to go
15:03 on to TikTok, you want to go on to
15:04 YouTube, you want to be going on
15:06 Instagram Reels and having a look at the
15:08 videos that get the most views and have
15:11 a look at what it is that they're doing
15:14 in those hooks. And the last point on
15:16 storytelling is
15:18 what you say is infinitely more
15:21 important than how you say it. Most
15:24 people spend the majority of their time
15:26 finessing words on how they are saying a
15:30 particular thing. When instead what you
15:32 want to do is you want to focus on
15:34 saying the right things in the first
15:37 place. And the way that you do that is
15:39 you look at customer service complaints,
15:42 you look at reviews, you be in Facebook
15:44 groups, you look at YouTube comments,
15:46 and you really understand what that
15:48 beating pulse is of your marketplace.
15:52 So, that when you write words, they
15:54 strike like lightning cuz they
15:56 specifically talk to the pain points of
15:59 your marketplace. Once you know that
16:01 you're saying the right thing, then
16:04 think about saying it in the the best
16:07 way possible. Point number six, the very
16:10 very first sale that you will make is
16:12 the sale for somebody's attention. And
16:15 we're living in a day and age where
16:17 attention is a new form of currency. We
16:19 all hear that all the time. You're no
16:20 longer competing with Bob down the
16:22 street that's in a similar business to
16:24 you. You're competing with all the other
16:26 alternatives that possibly exist for
16:28 your prospect to spend their time and
16:29 attention on. Netflix, Instagram,
16:32 YouTube, everything is on the phone,
16:35 right? And this thing is the arena
16:39 that you must compete in to get people's
16:41 attention. And if your stuff is boring
16:44 and dull, then you are going to be dead
16:47 on arrival. And if you look at what the
16:49 biggest brands in the world are doing
16:51 and how they stand out, I used to like
16:53 look at them and think like like
16:56 how is this going to sell anything? Like
16:58 this this is this is the most outrageous
17:00 ad. It has nothing to do with even
17:01 selling the product in the first place.
17:04 And the thing that I didn't understand
17:05 at the time is the game that these guys
17:07 were playing is that they were winning
17:09 the first game, which is to get
17:11 attention and to get people to consume
17:15 the message in the first place. Is this
17:17 piece of marketing communication, this
17:19 ad, this video, this promo, this subject
17:22 line,
17:23 is it enough that it is worthy of my
17:26 customer's attention and it is greater
17:28 for them to consume than all the other
17:30 alternatives that exist that are
17:32 probably infinitely more entertaining
17:35 than what it is that you've got to sell?
17:37 And if you can't fundamentally answer
17:39 that with a big resounding yes, then you
17:42 have already pushing the boulder up the
17:45 hill. And the thing that you can do to
17:47 get the most amount of throughput in
17:50 your whole funnel with everything that
17:52 you do is just to increase consumption.
17:55 And the best way to increase consumption
17:57 is to by definition have something that
17:58 is entertaining, to have strong hooks,
18:01 and very good packaging to that content.
18:03 Point number seven is building desire
18:06 versus selling. Marketing's job is to
18:09 make sales redundant. You should be
18:12 building so much desire in your
18:15 marketplace that people are already sold
18:19 before you even ask them to buy. You
18:22 build enough desire that people are
18:25 coming to you and they're lining up
18:28 asking for help for what it is that
18:30 you're selling. Think about an Apple
18:32 shop, right? You don't have to ever go
18:34 into an Apple shop and the person there
18:35 is like aggressively selling you a
18:37 MacBook Pro. Like the people that are in
18:40 an Apple shop, they're just order
18:42 takers. They have earned that right.
18:44 They have built enough desire by
18:46 creating incredible products that solve
18:49 pressing problems in the most elegant
18:51 way that exists, they can charge a
18:54 premium for doing so. And there's so
18:55 much desire around that that people are
18:59 literally, by definition, lined up out
19:02 the front of their stores to buy their
19:05 products at a premium. And that's a good
19:07 telltale sign that they are absolute
19:10 masters at not only creating an
19:13 exceptional product, but they are also
19:16 exceptional marketers. That is literally
19:19 your job as a marketer to be
19:21 articulating the pain points, showing
19:24 them how your product solves them,
19:26 agitating those pain points, and then
19:28 presenting your offer as something that
19:30 is truly unique and exciting and unlike
19:34 anything that they've ever seen or heard
19:36 about before. So, whenever you're in
19:38 those situations and people aren't
19:41 willingly handing over money, just
19:43 understand that the reason that they're
19:46 not doing it is because you simply
19:48 aren't good enough yet and you haven't
19:51 built enough desire in your marketplace.
19:53 And you're either not marketing enough
19:56 or the marketing isn't effective. Point
19:58 number eight, pricing. Price is what you
20:01 pay and value is what you get to quote
20:04 Warren Buffett. And a transaction takes
20:06 place where value exceeds price.
20:11 And in business, you really want to get
20:13 into a situation where you have way more
20:15 demand than supply of what it is that
20:17 you've got. And then you play with the
20:19 price elasticity equilibrium. I.e. You
20:23 figure out where on the demand curve do
20:26 you need to price your product so that
20:28 you can fulfill and you can able to
20:31 actually meet the demand for what it is
20:33 that you've got. And you have enough
20:36 supply to meet that demand at the
20:38 highest premium possible. Most people
20:40 when it comes to pricing, they look at
20:42 how much it costs to deliver that
20:45 product or service and then they mark up
20:48 a multiple of what they think is
20:50 reasonable. This is the worst thing that
20:52 you can do.
20:54 Because a lot of the time when people do
20:56 that, they never factor in all the other
20:58 overheads that exist in order to acquire
21:01 a customer. The ads, the sales team, the
21:03 product innovation team, the packaging,
21:06 inflation, all of those things. Instead,
21:09 what you want to do is you want to
21:11 prescribe a value
21:14 to the problem that your product solves.
21:16 And that is the thing that you want to
21:18 think about what it would be worth to my
21:21 customer in order to help them solve
21:24 that problem. The more money that you
21:25 make, the more that you can invest in
21:28 your product and service. The more
21:29 people that you can hire. The more
21:31 innovation that you can spend on to
21:33 solve that problem in a better way for
21:36 more and more people. Which brings us to
21:38 the next point, the chef versus the
21:41 business builder.
21:43 Most people, they start their business
21:45 because they are the chef that loves
21:48 cooking. They think, "Hey, I really love
21:50 cooking. Why don't I start a catering
21:52 business?" They think that their love
21:54 for what it is is enough for them to
21:57 build a business. And there's an
21:59 underbelly to this. They are the person
22:01 julienning the carrots and scrubbing the
22:03 potatoes. They're also plating up the
22:06 food. They're doing all the accounts.
22:08 They're doing the tax returns. They're
22:10 doing all the errands that need to be
22:12 done within their business.
22:14 And they end up overworked and underpaid
22:17 for what it is that they're doing and
22:18 the amount of stress that they're
22:19 burdening from themselves. They get
22:21 basically put at a fork in a road, which
22:23 is do I continue doing what I'm doing
22:25 right now, which is very very stressful,
22:27 or do I hire people to help me come in
22:31 and grow this business. And the problem
22:33 is what got you here is not going to get
22:36 you there. And the skills that you do of
22:39 being on the tools and doing all of
22:41 those things, you realize as you start
22:42 to bring people into your business,
22:44 like, "Hey, like I can't do this anymore
22:47 if I want the business to grow." And I
22:48 need to start focusing on things that
22:51 actually move the money needle and bring
22:54 enough cash into the business in order
22:56 for me to hire people, to train people,
22:59 to retain people, to lead people. And
23:02 your skill set goes from being the
23:03 practitioner to actually being the
23:06 business builder that is responsible for
23:08 growing this business. And they are two
23:10 entirely different skill sets. There is
23:12 like this place that I call Death
23:14 Valley. And it's where you as the
23:16 business owner are doing absolutely
23:18 everything that is required in your
23:19 business yourself. You're working very
23:21 very long hours. You're very very
23:23 stressed. Yeah, you're making good
23:25 money, but you don't have any time to
23:27 even spend that money or spend with your
23:29 family or do any of those things because
23:32 you're so busy doing all of this stuff.
23:34 And then you're like, "Shit, this is not
23:37 the reason I got into business. I got
23:38 into business because it was going to be
23:40 like lots of freedom and I'd be making
23:42 more money and I might have location
23:44 independence." But then you realize that
23:46 that's not the case. And then you're
23:47 like, "Oh, what do I do? Okay, I need to
23:48 hire some people." All right, in order
23:51 to hire people, I need to have enough
23:53 money to hire those people. And then if
23:56 I go and hire them and then I'm going to
23:58 be spending all my time training those
24:00 people, then who's going to be
24:02 delivering for my clients? Like, how am
24:04 I going to do that? And it's the chicken
24:05 and the egg. It's a a cycle that I see
24:07 all businesses have to go through and it
24:09 is very character forming and you learn
24:12 a lot about yourself and it becomes very
24:14 very stressful and you need to
24:17 understand that is typically a season or
24:19 a chapter.
24:20 And it's not going to be forever and you
24:21 need to quickly get in and get out of
24:25 that situation. And the way that you do
24:27 that is focusing on the activities in
24:29 your business that are revenue
24:31 producing. What are the things that are
24:32 going to ring the cash register, bring
24:34 in customers so that you have enough
24:36 funds to go and get higher people, build
24:38 a team. And then as you build the team,
24:40 they could become more competent, help
24:41 you come in and run the business. And
24:43 then you can focus more on the revenue
24:46 producing activities. The better that
24:47 you become as a marketer, the more
24:50 highly leveraged tasks that you'll be
24:51 working on. In the beginning, that might
24:53 be the one-to-one interactions of
24:55 getting customers and then it might be
24:57 marketing and running ads and then it
24:59 will be like, "Oh, you're focused on
25:01 your employer brand." You always want to
25:04 look at how much is a unit of time worth
25:07 to you. Take the total amount of profit
25:10 that you earn in a month or in a year
25:12 and divide that by the number of hours
25:14 that you work in a month or year. And
25:17 you do not want to be doing anything
25:19 that is below that hourly rate that you
25:21 could then go out and hire somebody to
25:23 do those things. Point number 10, take
25:26 big swings. The more success that you
25:28 get, the more money that you accrue, the
25:30 more that you will have the ability to
25:32 take big swings. And in the beginning,
25:35 success is usually by taking lots of
25:37 smaller swings that aren't going to put
25:39 you out of business. But the more
25:41 financial resources that you get is you
25:43 want what others don't have, then you
25:45 need to do what others don't do. And the
25:48 best way to do that is to take big,
25:50 violent, wild swings of something that
25:52 no one else in your marketplace is
25:54 doing, nothing else that you've ever
25:56 done before. Those are the things that
25:58 are really going to give you the most
26:00 disproportionate results to what it is
26:02 that you're doing. The more years that I
26:04 get under my belt in business, the more
26:06 I realize that the bigger swings that I
26:08 take,
26:09 the further that I can hit the ball.
26:11 Point number 11, master one channel. In
26:14 marketing, you've got demand capture and
26:17 you've got demand generation. And you do
26:20 not want to start by trying to master
26:23 both of those things straight out of the
26:25 gate.
26:26 If there are people that are actively
26:28 searching for what it is that you sell,
26:30 the easiest path for you to go down is
26:32 demand capture. I.e. Are people on
26:35 Google actively looking for a solution
26:38 to what it is that you provide? They are
26:39 the easiest people to convert. You do
26:42 not need to have crazy offers, hooks,
26:44 and storytelling or anything to be able
26:46 to convince those people to buy. They
26:48 are customers that I call have a
26:49 bleeding neck and they're looking for an
26:51 immediate solution to that problem.
26:53 Demand capture are where the millions
26:56 are made. But demand generation are
26:59 where the billions are made. And that is
27:01 being able to go out into a marketplace
27:03 and to generate demand, to be able to
27:05 put out ads, offers, hooks, and stories
27:09 that actually gets people to want what
27:13 is that you've got. So think Facebook
27:15 ads, YouTube ads. And this is the
27:17 channel that unlocks the most violent
27:19 scale in any business because you're not
27:21 limited by the number of people that are
27:23 specifically searching for what it is
27:25 that you've got. It also requires a much
27:27 greater level of skill because you need
27:29 to take somebody that is not remotely
27:31 even interested in what it is that
27:33 you've got and you need to pique their
27:35 interest in order to then provide a
27:36 solution to what it is that you've got
27:39 and actually get those people to buy.
27:41 Which brings us to point number 12, the
27:43 larger market formula. Basically, what
27:46 it shows you is that in any given
27:48 market, there are 3% of people that are
27:51 looking to buy. They're the people that
27:53 already know that they've got a problem.
27:55 They're on Google. They're looking for
27:57 solutions to their problems. Then you
27:59 have a further 17% of the market.
28:02 They're the guys that are in information
28:04 gathering mode. They kind of out there
28:07 seeking out information. They might not
28:08 be looking to buy right now, but they're
28:10 certainly thinking about it and they're
28:11 they're open to it. Then you have a
28:13 further 20% of the market that is
28:16 problem aware. They know that they have
28:19 a problem. They know that they need to
28:20 lose weight. They need to buy a car.
28:22 They need to meet a partner. But they're
28:24 not actively out there seeking out
28:27 information and looking to buy. And then
28:29 you have 60% of the market that is not
28:33 problem aware. And that's where it's
28:35 your skill as a marketer in the demand
28:37 generation to be able to go out to those
28:40 people and to be able to get their
28:42 attention, to hook them in, to keep them
28:45 interested, to build enough desire in
28:48 what it is that you've got, and then
28:50 make them an offer and get them to buy.
28:52 Once you master the skill in being able
28:54 to do that, that is when you unlock a
28:57 different level of geometric growth for
28:59 your business. Step number 13, quick
29:01 fast money versus big slow money. The
29:05 longer your time horizon, the bigger the
29:09 payoff is.
29:10 And if you're focused on making as much
29:13 money as you possibly can right now,
29:16 then you are going to be leaving a huge
29:18 amount of money on the table because
29:21 you're not playing the long game. And
29:24 longer games have fewer players in them.
29:27 And you want to play in decades, not in
29:30 years. But most people, they're just so
29:33 impatient that they want that quick win.
29:36 That they're just concerned only about
29:38 how much money that they're going to
29:39 make this month or this year. Where if
29:42 you look at the biggest businesses in
29:44 the world, take for example Amazon. They
29:48 lost money
29:50 for over a decade before they started
29:53 making money. They had such a long-term
29:55 perspective of being able to build out a
29:58 logistic hub of their business that
30:01 would allow them to reach all the
30:04 customers at the scale that they are
30:06 today. And that required a significant
30:09 investment.
30:10 But because they were willing to do
30:12 that, they get to reap the biggest
30:15 money, the big slow money. And you want
30:18 to in the beginning be focused on quick
30:19 fast money because you need money. It's
30:21 the oxygen that keeps, you know, the
30:23 lungs of your business full. But the
30:26 more success that you see and the more
30:27 money that you kind of squirrel away,
30:30 then that earns you the right to be able
30:32 to play longer games and really think
30:34 about not where do you want to be this
30:37 time next year? Where do you want to be
30:38 in 10 years? What is the type of
30:40 business that you want to be building?
30:42 And when you start thinking that
30:44 long-term, it completely changes your
30:47 perspective of the things that you
30:49 invest in in your business. I learned
30:51 very early on in my career that people
30:56 don't want to get rich.
30:58 They want to be rich. You want to go
31:01 and fish where the fewest go because the
31:03 fishing is best where the fewest go. And
31:06 the fewest go where those long-term
31:10 games, where you're playing in decades.
31:12 But the payoff for those things is going
31:14 to be infinitely more than the whole
31:16 pool of people that are playing for the
31:18 quick small money. Point number 14,
31:21 focus on the skills that have the
31:24 longest half-life. In business, there
31:27 are infinite things that you can focus
31:29 on.
31:30 And
31:31 the more success that you see,
31:34 the more that you want to engineer your
31:36 week, your month, and your years to be
31:38 focused on acquiring the skills that are
31:42 going to compound throughout your whole
31:44 career. So, as a marketer, you might
31:46 start as the salesperson. You might
31:49 start as the media buyer that is running
31:51 the Facebook ads, that is in Facebook
31:53 ads all day long. What we know from
31:54 Facebook ads now is going to be entirely
31:57 different likely in the next 4 years.
31:59 So, if I obsess over the next year of
32:01 becoming the best Facebook media buyer
32:04 in the world, those skills and all the
32:07 time that I spend to acquire them, they
32:09 are going to be completely different in
32:11 4 years. Therefore, they have a very
32:14 small half-life. Instead, leading
32:17 people, communicating with people, being
32:20 a clear writer, these are all things
32:24 that are going to continually compound
32:26 in whatever the skills or whatever the
32:28 business is that you run in the future.
32:32 And as you continue to work on those
32:34 skills, the biggest hack that I have
32:36 found is just to try to make yourself
32:39 redundant at every step of the journey
32:42 so that you can focus on those skills
32:45 that give you the most leverage
32:46 long-term and that are going to serve
32:48 you because if they give you the most
32:49 leverage, you're going to get the most
32:51 throughput and you're going to build a
32:53 much bigger opportunity for everybody
32:55 that's involved. Point number 15, spend
32:58 80% of your time focusing on how to
33:02 increase the average lifetime of a
33:04 customer and spend 20% of your time
33:07 focusing on how to reduce your customer
33:10 acquisition cost. If there's anything
33:13 that I see more common in the marketing
33:16 world is broke marketers obsessing on
33:20 how to lower their cost per lead or to
33:23 lower their customer acquisition cost.
33:25 And they obsessively run crazy split
33:28 tests, they're buying every single
33:30 Facebook media buying course that exist
33:32 out there, and they're just myopically
33:35 focused on this thing like it's the
33:36 detriment to their business.
33:38 And instead, what you want to do is
33:41 focus 80% of your time focusing on how
33:45 do I deliver a better product or service
33:47 that either gets people to buy more or
33:50 to buy more frequently? And just provide
33:53 an incredible experience where my
33:55 customer's lifetime value is higher than
33:59 anybody else in my industry or my
34:02 category. And therefore, I don't need to
34:04 obsessively be focused on how do I have
34:06 the cheapest customer acquisition cost?
34:08 I can afford
34:10 to spend the most to acquire a customer
34:14 because I make the most off each
34:16 customer. And you're able just to snatch
34:19 up everyone in that marketplace because
34:21 you're not worried about trying to shave
34:23 two or three dollars off your CPA. 80%
34:25 on increasing LTV and 20% on reducing
34:30 your customer acquisition cost. Point
34:32 number 16, advanced people always do the
34:36 basics. I see people all the time
34:38 obsessed over doing the most advanced
34:41 and shiny things as possible. And
34:43 whenever I used to read like biographies
34:45 of billionaires or I'd meet with people
34:48 and I would ask them about their
34:49 journey, I would feel like that they're
34:52 leaving out all the juice. I was like,
34:54 "When are they going to get to the
34:55 tactical stuff? Like the exact sentences
34:58 that I can say, the exact ads that I can
35:01 run that are going to make me
35:03 successful?" And they always spoke in
35:06 like generalities and like in
35:08 frameworks. And I was like,
35:11 "Yeah, like that's all good, but like
35:12 give me the juice, dude. Like give me
35:14 the actual specific things." Like,
35:17 "Yeah, okay, cool. Like have an
35:18 exceptional product, right? And have it
35:21 so exceptional that you can charge
35:23 premiums and then make more profit per
35:26 customer so that you can hire the best
35:28 people to come in and help you with the
35:30 business and make sure that you break
35:32 off a percentage of your annual profits
35:35 into R&D." I was like, "Yeah, that's all
35:37 cool, right?
35:39 Like give me the tactics now. Give me
35:41 the shiny tactics." And I realized after
35:45 reading lots of books, speaking with
35:47 lots of people, these advanced people,
35:49 they always did the fundamentals every
35:51 time.
35:52 Whereas you look at like a lot of gurus
35:54 online and if you look at a lot of
35:57 business experts, they will be selling
35:59 you the most advanced thing, the most
36:01 advanced tactic. And a lot of the times
36:03 they don't have big businesses because
36:05 they're not following the fundamentals.
36:08 They're obsessed over the most advanced
36:10 funnel that you can have or the new
36:12 Facebook ad targeting strategy. I can
36:14 save you all the heartache right now.
36:17 None of that matters. If you just
36:20 do the fundamentals and you do them
36:23 exceptionally well, you will wipe the
36:26 floor
36:27 with the people that have the most
36:29 complex advanced funnels and ad
36:31 targeting strategies that exist. Point
36:33 number 17, skepticism. In the 17 years
36:37 that I have been in business, one of the
36:39 constants that I have seen is that the
36:41 level of skepticism in all markets keeps
36:45 on increasing. Now that you have TikTok
36:48 and YouTube and all these organic
36:50 traffic engines that exist, the barriers
36:52 to entry to start a business keep on
36:55 getting lower and lower. Then on the
36:57 other end of that, you have the whole
36:59 guru model where there's a whole bunch
37:01 of people out there that are selling
37:03 courses about people quitting their 9-5
37:05 and starting their own business. You
37:07 have more people than are not experts
37:10 that are in business in every single
37:12 category that exist. And as a result of
37:15 that, there is a lot of incompetence.
37:18 And that incompetence adds to people's
37:20 bad experience, which also adds to their
37:23 skepticism. People are just jaded.
37:25 They're more skeptical than ever before.
37:29 And you need to be very, very present of
37:32 that in all of the marketing that you
37:34 put out there.
37:36 And the antidote to skepticism is value.
37:40 And the thing that I have found that you
37:42 want to focus on is by proving to people
37:45 that you can help them by actually
37:47 helping them. There is nothing that you
37:49 can do, there is no headline hack,
37:51 there's no ad targeting hack that is
37:53 going to trump that. And when you do
37:55 that, you get a much larger group of
37:58 your market that actually does buy
38:00 because you lower that skepticism meter
38:03 of them being burnt in the past and you
38:04 provide value and you prove to people
38:06 that you can help them by actually
38:08 helping them. Then in addition to that,
38:10 you also provide all the goodwill to
38:13 your marketplace by helping people that
38:15 are never ever going to buy off you as
38:17 well. So, you need to throw stones at
38:20 the other alternatives that exist
38:23 that aren't your product and you need to
38:25 show how those products have not been
38:29 able to deliver on the promise that they
38:31 make in the first place, which forms
38:33 into something called a unique
38:35 mechanism. So, we'll take the weight
38:38 loss market, right? If someone's in the
38:40 weight loss market, the chances are that
38:42 they have tried another diet, they've
38:44 tried paleo, keto, intermittent fasting.
38:49 They've tried all of those things, yet
38:51 here they are still overweight and
38:54 wanting to lose the weight.
38:56 And you need to be able to articulate
38:59 why all of those things have failed the
39:01 individual. And then and only then, then
39:04 do you present the unique mechanism
39:07 behind the solution, which is your
39:09 product, to solve that problem. And you
39:12 need to compare and contrast and show
39:15 people how it is superior to all the
39:18 other alternatives that exist in the
39:20 marketplace. And unless that you're
39:22 doing that with your ads and your
39:24 funnels and your promos, then you're
39:27 never ever addressing the skepticism
39:30 that is rampant in the market. And
39:32 unless you address that skepticism, your
39:34 conversions will always be low. Point
39:37 number 18, a godfather offer. A
39:41 compelling offer is infinitely more
39:45 powerful than a convincing argument. In
39:47 any transaction, there is risk present.
39:50 And in most transaction, the business
39:53 owner is asking the prospect to wear all
39:57 of that risk. They're like, "This is the
39:59 product, just buy it. This is the price,
40:02 just buy it." When realistically, you
40:06 want to be aware of the risk that is
40:08 present in every transaction, and you
40:11 want to burden the majority of that risk
40:14 as a business owner. And the more that
40:17 you do that, and the more clearly
40:18 defined the end benefit that your offer
40:21 enables, the more compelling your offer
40:24 is. Now, the biggest rebuttal that I get
40:26 when it comes to making a Godfather
40:28 offer is that like, "Oh, wow, you want
40:31 me to burden all of that risk? And then
40:34 if I do that, there might be refunds,
40:36 and that's going to cost me a lot of
40:37 money." But the thing that most business
40:40 owners don't take into consideration is
40:42 that we are all in the business of going
40:45 out there and getting attention and then
40:46 converting that attention into
40:48 customers. The more compelling the
40:50 offer, the more people that you're going
40:51 to get to actually convert, and that is
40:54 going to lower your customer acquisition
40:56 cost. A compelling offer is more so
40:58 about having a very clearly defined
41:02 promise that you're making, and then
41:04 removing all the risk. And my litmus
41:07 test for this is if the offer does not
41:11 keep the founder up at night, then it
41:13 simply isn't strong enough. Think about
41:16 what is it that you can add to make your
41:18 offer more compelling, or how more
41:20 clearly defined can you make it, or how
41:23 much more risk can you burden for your
41:26 prospects to make this a no-brainer for
41:28 them. Do that and watch your sales
41:30 explode. Point number 19, showmanship
41:34 and service. I believe that showmanship
41:37 is one of the most long-lost art forms
41:40 that exist in business.
41:42 People will forget about what you say in
41:44 your ads, what you write in your emails,
41:49 what's in the specific thing that it is
41:51 that you've got, but they won't forget
41:54 about the way that you made them feel.
41:56 Not only just being a showman in your
41:59 promotion and in your ads and in the way
42:01 that you get attention, but even in the
42:03 way that you service, delight, and
42:05 surprise your customers. And a great
42:07 example of this is the Ritz-Carlton.
42:11 They're kind of been studied as like the
42:13 gold standard of customer service. And
42:16 one of the things that they've got is
42:18 that they give every single team member
42:20 in their business an allowance of $2,000
42:24 for each incident, not each person
42:28 staying in their hotel, each incident to
42:31 right a wrong or a complaint that a
42:34 customer has while staying with them.
42:36 They don't need to get approval, they
42:37 all have $2,000 as a budget to right
42:40 that. And as a result of that, that just
42:44 has people having the most incredible
42:46 experience ever when they stay at their
42:49 hotels because if anything ever goes
42:51 wrong, that person that is serving them
42:53 has the authority and power to spend
42:55 $2,000 to make sure that that experience
42:59 is no longer an issue, and use that
43:01 opportunity
43:03 not to just overcome that problem, but
43:05 to surprise and delight the customer.
43:07 Not thinking about what is reasonable,
43:10 what can you deliver on, start with the
43:12 extremes, and then you think about how
43:15 can I inject some of that in my
43:17 business. What is it that I would be
43:18 able to do to surprise and delight my
43:21 customers so that they have a remarkable
43:25 experience that they by definition want
43:28 to go and leave a remark about and tell
43:30 other people, and then you get more
43:32 customers. And I have found that the
43:34 more showmanship that I inject in my
43:37 business, from the way that I promote
43:39 and market to how we service and delight
43:42 customers once they come in, there is
43:44 nothing quite like lighting someone up
43:47 with a big smile or getting handwritten
43:49 letters of saying, "What a surprise and
43:52 how delighted that they are." It's also
43:55 good business, which brings us to AI and
43:58 the future of marketing. Not a day goes
44:01 by where I don't receive an email, a
44:04 handwritten letter, or somebody asking
44:08 me
44:09 sub- wait, "What about AI? Like how is
44:12 Isn't this just going to make all
44:13 agencies redundant? Isn't this going to
44:15 make most businesses redundant? What are
44:17 you focusing on as a marketer as AI
44:20 comes about, and what do you think is
44:22 the areas that I need to focus on that
44:25 aren't going to be replaced by AI?" And
44:28 I think that over the last couple of
44:29 years, like I have been completely in
44:31 the trenches on AI and applying it to my
44:36 clients' businesses, to my businesses.
44:37 And the more that I use it, and the more
44:40 that I have my team use it, things
44:42 become apparent. A lot of the manual
44:44 tasks that we do, like interpreting
44:46 data, operating within these auction
44:49 pools on Facebook and Google Ads and
44:51 doing media buying, all of that stuff is
44:54 eventually and inevitably going to be
44:56 completely automated.
44:58 But if everybody is using AI for all
45:01 their media buying
45:03 and all of these marketing campaigns
45:05 that they're running, then what is going
45:08 to be the differentiator? What is going
45:10 to give people an edge if everybody has
45:13 the same advantage? And that's been a
45:15 question that I've been obsessing over
45:16 for the last year and a half.
45:18 And the thing that I keep on coming back
45:21 to
45:22 is it's all about the ideas. And
45:26 imagination
45:28 is more important than knowledge, to
45:31 quote Albert Einstein. And I think that
45:33 that is going to be something that's
45:34 going to ring true over the decades to
45:37 come. And it's going to be people's
45:39 ability to come up with big ideas and to
45:42 apply their imagination that then we can
45:44 guide AI into to help us do all the
45:48 logistical script writing, media buying,
45:51 running the ads, writing the headlines,
45:53 and doing all of those things.
45:55 And when it comes to like ideas and
45:58 creativity, the thing that I have found
46:01 that helped that is that solitude is the
46:04 wind that stokes the flame of
46:07 creativity.
46:08 And you do not want to be in a constant
46:10 state of consumption because the more
46:13 that you consume, the less that you
46:15 create.
46:16 So, if you want to create more, you have
46:19 to consume
46:20 fewer things. And when you allow time
46:23 and space to transpire, and you allow
46:26 quiet to happen, and you go for a walk
46:28 without your phone, that's often the
46:30 time when your imagination runs wild.
46:32 It's these big ideas and these concepts
46:35 that is going to be really the
46:37 differentiator with the coming of AI.
46:39 So, I hope this serves you wherever
46:41 you're at on your business journey.
46:43 Like, subscribe, and I'll see you in the
46:45 next one.

1:57:59 saves it in the cache. If connection
1:58:02 fails, it clears the cache so retries

2:10:04 collaborate. So first of all what is
2:10:06 inest? Well, when building production
2:10:09 ready apps, speed and reliability are
2:10:12 key because your users shouldn't have to
2:10:14 wait while your app processes tasks like
2:10:17 sending emails, updating notifications,
2:10:20 or handling any other background work.
2:10:22 And that's where in comes in. It runs
2:10:25 all of these tasks in the background so
2:10:27 that users get a smooth experience. A
2:10:30 couple of things that make in so
2:10:32 powerful is one it lets us run
2:10:34 background jobs like for example sending
2:10:36 emails without slowing down the user
2:10:39 experience and if something fails it
2:10:41 automatically retries it so that it
2:10:43 won't just disappear and just will try
2:10:45 again until it succeeds and it scales
2:10:48 all the way from doing one job to doing
2:10:50 millions of jobs. So to set it up click
2:10:52 the link down in the description to be
2:10:54 able to follow along and see exactly

3:52:29 And whenever someone starts typing
3:52:31 something out, we want to display the
3:52:33 stock's result being picked up from the
3:52:36 API. Now, here's the thing. Every single
3:52:39 keystroke that a user makes will
3:52:41 re-trigger the API call and make another
3:52:44 request, which can slow down our entire
3:52:46 application. And that's the perfect use
3:52:48 case for a use debounce hook. You can
3:52:52 think of it like a little delay button.
3:52:54 So, let's go ahead and implement it
3:52:56 together, and I'll explain how it works.
3:52:58 So head over within the hooks folder and
3:53:01 then create a new file which you can
3:53:04 call use debounce.ts.
3:53:08 Within it we will export a new function
3:53:11 called use debounce. And you can know
3:53:14 that it's a hook because it starts with
3:53:15 the word use. It'll accept a callback
3:53:19 function that we want to debounce. In
3:53:21 this case, that's going to be a search
3:53:23 and it'll be of a type function that
3:53:25 returns void, meaning doesn't return
3:53:27 anything. It simply updates the state.
3:53:30 And we also want to pass a delay for how
3:53:33 long will it wait before it gets
3:53:35 recalled. That'll be of a type number.
3:53:37 So instead of firing a function right
3:53:40 away, it will wait until a certain
3:53:42 amount of time has passed, like 300
3:53:44 milliseconds. And why does this matter?
3:53:47 Well, if you take our search command,
3:53:49 normally as you start typing AAPL for
3:53:52 Apple, your app will try to make four
3:53:55 separate API calls. One for A, then one
3:53:59 for AA, third one for AAP, and finally
3:54:02 the final one for AAPL. That's a lot of
3:54:05 unnecessary traffic. With the bounce,
3:54:08 the hook says, "Hold on. Let's wait
3:54:10 until the user is done typing and then
3:54:13 after 300 milliseconds, we'll fire just
3:54:15 one clean API request. To make this
3:54:18 work, we'll use two built-in hooks from
3:54:21 React. So, I'll import them. The first
3:54:24 one is use callback and the second one
3:54:27 is use ref coming from React and we'll
3:54:31 also give it a use client directive
3:54:33 because we're using browser
3:54:35 functionalities in this custom hook.
3:54:37 Then I will define a timeout ref by
3:54:41 making it equal to use ref at the start
3:54:44 equal to null. And I will return a use
3:54:47 call back
3:54:49 with a callback function that'll check
3:54:52 if timeout ref.curren
3:54:55 is there. Then it'll simply clear this
3:54:57 timeout based on the current ref. And
3:55:00 finally we'll set the timeout ref
3:55:03 to be equal to set timeout. and we'll
3:55:06 pass in the call back function and the
3:55:08 delay. Let's spell it properly. Time
3:55:11 out. And we will recall this whenever
3:55:14 the call back or the delay change. We
3:55:18 can also specify the type of this
3:55:20 reference and say that it'll be a
3:55:22 Node.js
3:55:24 timeout
3:55:27 or maybe it'll be a null at the start.
3:55:29 So now it'll know that this exists on
3:55:32 it. This was supposed to be NodeJS like
3:55:34 this. Perfect. So now we can update our
3:55:38 search command to use this new callback
3:55:41 functionality by implementing it within
3:55:44 a handle search function. So const
3:55:46 handle search is going to be an
3:55:48 asynchronous function which will run the
3:55:51 query or if there's no input it'll reset
3:55:53 it to the initial stocks. So I'll say if
3:55:56 it's not currently in the search mode.

Digital Marketing 101 (A Beginner’s Guide To Marketing In 2026)

0:00 Hey, my friend Adam here. In this video,
0:01 I'm going to help you seriously level up

11:30 consumers homes and enticing them to
11:32 make a purchase Performance Marketing is
11:34 an online strategy where advertisers can
11:36 only pay when specific actions are
11:38 completed these actions can include
11:40 sales leads or clicks it allows
11:42 businesses to achieve measurable results
11:44 while minimizing unnecessary marketing
11:46 costs a classic performance marketing
11:48 strategy is employed by Amazon through
11:50 its affiliate program the online retail
11:52 giant pays commissions to affiliate
11:54 websites to drive traffic or sales on
11:56 its platform ensuring that they only pay
11:58 for successful referrals inbound

57:34 Impressions will be greater than the

1:20:06 refers to an individual who has chosen

1:20:09 website or social media channel and made
1:20:11 an effort to communicate with us they
1:20:13 may have filled out a contact form
1:20:15 subscribed to a newsletter or even made
1:20:17 a phone call essentially Elite is
1:20:19 someone who has shown interest in our
1:20:20 products or services and provided us
1:20:22 with their contact information which is
1:20:24 then added to our database an essential
1:20:26 aspect of a lead is the concept of
1:20:28 explicit consent it's crucial that the
1:20:30 individual gives us clear permission to

1:20:34 convert a visitor into a lead you need a

1:20:40 where the user leaves their contact
1:20:42 information the evolution of lead
1:20:43 generation the concept of leads has
1:20:45 existed for many years even before the
1:20:47 digital era previously leads were

1:20:51 interest in a product or service however
1:20:53 with the Advent of digital marketing the

1:20:56 potentially generate multiplied
1:20:58 exponentially sometimes into the
1:20:59 hundreds or thousands every day this
1:21:01 abundance of leads created a need for a
1:21:03 more nuanced classification system to
1:21:05 distinguish between various levels of
1:21:07 interest and Readiness to purchase
1:21:09 classifying leads mqls and sqls to
1:21:12 better manage and categorize leads
1:21:13 marketers develop two new terms
1:21:15 marketing qualified leads mqls and sales
1:21:18 qualified leads sqls marketing qualified
1:21:20 leads mqls an mql is is a lead who has
1:21:23 not only shown interest in your product
1:21:24 or service but also fits a certain set
1:21:26 of predefined criteria that make a
1:21:28 likely prospective customer this can
1:21:30 include a specific demographic or
1:21:32 Geographic attributes they're plac
1:21:34 within the business sector or even the

1:21:38 essentially an mql is a lead that

1:21:41 sales qualified leads sqls the SQL is
1:21:44 the next step up from an mql an SQL is a
1:21:47 lead who in addition to meeting all the

1:21:55 through various signals such as a
1:21:56 specific inquiry a direct request for
1:21:58 further information or even an expressed

1:22:05 powerful tool for businesses allowing
1:22:07 them to place leads on a spectrum of
1:22:09 Readiness to purchase at one end we have

1:22:22 leverage all of this information we

1:22:26 tool which will help you set up lead

1:28:37 constantly changing your social ads
1:28:39 campaigns social media platforms provide

1:28:44 performance however changing your ads
1:28:46 parameters frequently can skew data and
1:28:48 prevent you from obtaining clear

1:28:58 its brand consistency and confusion

1:29:07 adjustments number three launching a
1:29:09 campaign without establishing metrics to
1:29:11 track the results without proper metrics
1:29:14 there's no way to measure the success or
1:29:16 failure of a campaign take the example
1:29:18 of the fire Festival this luxury music
1:29:20 festival's promotion relied heavily on

1:29:28 Roi the result was a disastrous event
1:29:30 that was nothing like its online
1:29:32 portrayal leading to public outrage and
1:29:34 legal action number four having too many

1:29:44 to raise Capital attract new investors

1:29:55 marketing campaigns need a singular
1:29:57 clear Focus to be effective for example

1:30:20 sometimes neglect the importance of a be

1:30:24 example of Goog plus when it was first
1:30:25 launched Google didn't test its landing
1:30:28 page variations leading to initial low
1:30:30 engagement and usability issues had they

1:30:36 refined their approach with marketing
1:30:38 automation tools you can set up ab tests
1:30:41 that change small details on a landing
1:30:43 page from the call to action button to
1:30:45 the text encouraging the users to fill
1:30:47 the contact form keep in mind that each

1:30:51 exclusively on one change that way
1:30:53 you'll be able to isolate the user's

1:30:58 bring the winning option to all users
