## Source: how-i-would-learn-digital-marketing-if-i-could-start-over.txt

- Original path: `addintional info to add\how-i-would-learn-digital-marketing-if-i-could-start-over.txt`
- Size: `14439` bytes
- SHA256: `4A83F1B9F134FF2FD7C1558C2D6372DCD4795971DBD2DE37F1BA41835612874D`

---

How I Would Learn Digital Marketing (If I Could Start Over)

0:00 14 years ago, I started my career in digital marketing.
0:02 And throughout that time, I've built and sold a handful of online businesses, consulted
0:06 for small shops to celebrities to the Fortune 500, and I've been recognized in business
0:11 magazines like Forbes and Inc as an "SEO expert."
0:14 To top it off, the United States government considers me to be in the top 1% of my field.
0:19 Now, despite any success I've had in digital marketing, I've also made a ton of mistakes
0:24 along the way that cost me valuable time and money.
0:27 So today, I'm going to show you how I would learn digital marketing if I could start over
0:32 and I'll also share some of my biggest failures along the way.
0:35 Alright, so the very first thing I'd do is to choose just one area of digital marketing to focus on.
0:41 And this is the opposite of what I did.
0:43 So for context, in 2009, I started my first website and I basically spammed forums and classifieds.
0:49 That eventually slowed down, so I tried paid advertising.
0:52 And well… I wasted my money because I had no idea what I was doing.
0:55 But then social media was blowing up so I pivoted to that and tried to go viral with
1:00 coupon codes.
1:00 Boy was that embarrassing!
1:02 But still, I was making like a solid $2,000 a month.
1:06 I was officially a business man.
1:08 So I decided to hire a search engine optimization company because this is what we do!
1:14 And that was the turning point for me when my fire for SEO was ignited.
1:18 They scammed me for everything I had.
1:20 The good thing that came out of that though was that it fueled me with rage so much so
1:25 that I committed to focus on learning just SEO and to get really good at it so I wouldn't
1:31 have to hire another company.
1:32 And by around 2011, my company was worth over 7 figures and the main reason is because of SEO.
1:39 Now, by definition, digital marketing is just marketing that happens over the internet.
1:43 So you can choose to focus on just email marketing, pay-per-click advertising, search engine optimization,
1:49 social media marketing, etc.
1:51 Digital marketing is just so vast that it's impossible to become a master of all areas.
1:57 It's best to focus on just one area of internet marketing and to get really good at it.
2:02 Now, after you've chosen the area of digital marketing you want to focus on, you need to
2:06 make a mental commitment to learn as much as possible.
2:10 Now, while there isn't a single "right" way to learn digital marketing, what I've found
2:15 to be helpful is to learn in 3 stages.
2:17 So, for illustration purposes, let's say you chose SEO as your focus area.
2:22 Now, stage one is to focus on understanding the fundamentals.
2:25 So in SEO, that would include things like why and how SEO is important for businesses,
2:31 how search engines work, and the main ways SEO is done – so that includes keyword research,
2:37 link building, technical SEO and on-page SEO to name a few.
2:41 Fundamentals may not be exciting, but they're absolutely critical to learning, whether you're
2:46 learning an instrument, a new recipe, or digital marketing skills.
2:51 Stage two is all about making connections in how the fundamentals work together.
2:55 For example, you should be able to make the connection in SEO that content creation
3:00 is reliant on keyword research and that on-page SEO is connected with both your content writing
3:06 and keyword research.
3:08 Finally, stage three is all about learning how these techniques are executed – more specifically,
3:14 you'll want to get an idea of workflows and tools that you'll need to do your job.
3:19 For example, you should at least know that keyword research is done with a tool like
3:22 Keywords Explorer.
3:24 And the way people find keyword ideas is by starting with a broad seed, going to a keyword
3:29 ideas report, narrowing in on keywords with a set of filters, and then analyzing the search
3:35 engine results pages to understand if it's possible to compete.
3:39 These three stages are typically how courses are taught.
3:42 And for that reason, I think it's worth taking a reputable beginner's course on the area
3:47 of digital marketing you've chosen – like our free SEO fundamentals course.
3:51 This is something that I was too young and arrogant to do when I was getting started
3:55 with online marketing.
3:57 I went with the unguided route and literally… anytime someone said "advanced", I was like
4:02 Pavlov's dog and I'd give them all my attention.
4:05 I wasted tons of time and money because for some reason, I subconsciously thought it would
4:10 be more rewarding to figure it out on my own.
4:12 Now, as you go through this learning stage and consume content, there's going to be
4:16 a time when you feel like, "yeah, this all makes sense!"
4:20 But I can guarantee you that when you actually get to doing it, it's not going to
4:24 feel so straightforward anymore.
4:26 And that's why the next thing I recommend is to commit to actually practicing what you've learned.
4:32 In my opinion, there's no better way to learn digital marketing than to get first-hand experience.
4:37 And the best way to do that is to create your own site.
4:40 This way, you'll have a safe place to practice whatever and however you want.
4:45 Now, as you commit to actually doing something, I highly recommend doing two things.
4:49 #1. Cut yourself off from all new content.
4:54 You should have already consumed enough through a course or whatever resources that
4:58 you used to have a high-level view of what it looks like to execute on the marketing area you've chosen.
5:04 By cutting out new content, you'll be able to stay laser-focused on actually practicing
5:09 the fundamentals that will be an essential building block to your career.
5:14 And #2 don't obsess over things that aren't moving your progress forward.
5:18 For example, if you decide to build a hobby site so you can practice SEO, don't spend
5:23 hours trying to fine-tune your logo, fonts or blog layout.
5:28 As an SEO, you're going to want to focus on driving organic traffic through high rankings
5:33 in Google.
5:34 Now, if you're able to actually put all these things into practice, you will learn very quickly
5:39 how to actually get results with SEO or social media or email marketing or whatever area you
5:45 chose to focus on.
5:46 Now, if you want to know the fastest way to accelerate your growth and to put this "doing"
5:51 stage into overdrive, then I recommend getting a job or internship at an agency.
5:57 When you work at an agency, you get the unique experience of being able to work on a diverse
6:02 group of projects.
6:03 Not only is the exposure great, but it forces you to stretch that creativity muscle and to
6:08 apply your learning in various scenarios.
6:11 Now, perhaps the biggest advantage to working at an agency is that you get to be in the trenches
6:16 with people who are ahead of you in their digital marketing career.
6:20 You can ask them questions, watch them solve problems, and in some cases, people may be
6:25 willing to mentor you.
6:27 Thriving agencies are usually fast-paced environments, which obviously make them
6:31 a great place to learn digital marketing fast if that's your goal.
6:35 Now, while you obviously want to learn new skills as fast as possible, don't make
6:39 the mistake of taking shortcuts to get there.
6:42 And I'm talking from experience here.
6:44 Early in my career, I took an SEO shortcut that cost me seven-figures.
6:48 So my first company was an ecommerce store and I was driving the majority of its traffic through SEO.
6:53 Now, one of the hardest, but most important parts of SEO is getting backlinks to your pages.
6:59 And back in 2010 and 2011, backlinks were pretty much all you needed to rank a page
7:04 high in Google.
7:05 So I came across a private blog network called Build My Rank where I could buy virtually
7:10 an infinite number of backlinks.
7:12 And without fail, throwing a handful of links at each product page would boost it to the top of
7:18 Google for queries that were converting into tens of thousands of dollars every month.
7:23 The thing is, buying backlinks was and still is against Google's webmaster guidelines.
7:29 Now, while I was making a healthy living, I was getting ready to cash out.
7:32 But without warning, I lost like 85% of my traffic and a ton of revenue.
7:38 So when I sold the site a few months later, instead of getting a low seven-figure buyout,
7:43 I ended up getting, much, much, much, much less.
7:46 Now, had I focused on learning link building so I can build backlinks whenever I want,
7:51 not only would I have had a much nicer exit, but I would have been able to apply those
7:56 skills to pretty much every other SEO-driven business I created.
8:00 Bottomline: avoid shortcuts because they can be costly.
8:04 And you'll only really realize that in hindsight.
8:06 Now, another thing I would do differently is to actually build relationships with people.
8:10 So, for like the first 8 years of doing SEO, I literally didn't talk to anyone about what
8:16 I did because most people just didn't understand.
8:19 And it was lonely and I was really just stunting my professional growth.
8:23 So what I would do differently if I could start over is try and network with two different
8:28 groups of people.
8:29 So, Group 1 would be like-minded people who are trying to learn the same area of digital
8:34 marketing as you.
8:36 They're basically a safe place where you can share your ideas, experiences, and insights
8:41 without fear of judgment.
8:42 And not only can you learn from each other, but you'll also find motivation from each
8:47 other to get better at your craft.
8:49 In fact, this is what Mr. Beast did with a group of friends in his early stages of his YouTube career.
8:54 And in interviews, he frequently attributes his success to having this group of people
8:59 to grow with.
9:00 And the second group of people I'd network with are people you respect in your niche.
9:05 All it really takes is an email or a DM saying, "Hey, just wanted to let you know that I really
9:10 admire your work.
9:11 Thanks for all that you do!"
9:12 Now, that might sound weird, but at the end of the day, we're all just regular people.
9:17 In fact, in 2017 when I started sending these kinds of emails, I reached out to Tim Soulo,
9:22 CMO at Ahrefs, to just say that I respect what he's doing as a marketer – no agenda whatsoever.
9:28 To which he replied: "You've made my day!"
9:30 Fast forward one year, and I ended up working at Ahrefs.
9:34 Relationships in digital marketing are important.
9:36 In social media, these are people who can help amplify your work.
9:39 On YouTube, these are potential collaborators.
9:42 And in SEO, they're a critical part to link building.
9:46 Now, as you continue to build your own sites, work at agencies or other companies,
9:50 and network with like-minded people, you're going to come to a crossroad where you need to decide
9:57 what's next.
9:58 And I see three ways this can work – none of which I think are wrong or right.
10:02 So option 1, you can continue to stay a generalist in your focus area.
10:06 This means that you'll continue to build your knowledge and experience in most areas of
10:11 your specific field in digital marketing.
10:14 The pros with this is that generalists often become managers and leaders within companies
10:19 because they get more or less every part of what's being done.
10:23 The con is that you never really become an expert in anything, which obviously has its
10:29 own set of limitations.
10:31 Option 2, is to niche down further in your focus area.
10:34 So as you gain more knowledge and experience in your focus area, you're going to realize that
10:39 there's too much information to become a true expert in your field.
10:44 And for that reason, you may want to consider niching down further because each digital
10:50 marketing strategy breaks down quite a lot.
10:53 For example, if you've chosen to focus on social media, you may decide to focus strictly
10:57 on Facebook marketing.
10:59 Or perhaps you want to go more niche and choose Facebook group marketing for software companies.
11:05 Now, the pro of niching down is that it's much easier to become recognized as
11:09 a niche authority because there's less competition.
11:12 And if you're viewed as "that" authority, your reputation will likely be lasting and
11:17 you can typically demand higher wages.
11:20 The con is that there's usually less demand.
11:23 And option 3 is to expand into other areas of digital marketing.
11:27 And this is kind of the route that I've gone.
11:29 The way I recommend doing this is to expand into areas where your skills are complementary
11:34 and transferable.
11:35 For example, starting from SEO and expanding to content marketing is complementary and
11:41 the skills from SEO are totally transferable.
11:44 And even something like going from SEO to search ads would also be complementary
11:49 and some of those skills are transferable too.
11:51 Now, being 14 years into my digital marketing career, I can confidently tell you that I'm still
11:57 learning all the time.
11:59 And that's largely because I'm practicing a lot of the things that I just shared with you.
12:03 I work with a ton of smart people at Ahrefs in various departments who I love learning from.
12:08 I've had the opportunity to meet really smart people at conferences, through social media,
12:13 and even from random cold emails.
12:15 And I go through seasons of intentional learning, followed by intentional doing.
12:20 Now, while I've screwed up in many ways, I don't look at anything as a waste of time
12:25 because it helped me to get to where I am today.
12:27 I'm definitely not the smartest, richest, most recognized, or most successful digital marketer
12:33 in the world.
12:34 But this is a long journey that I've embraced along the way and I hope it's one that you'll
12:39 enjoy or are currently enjoying too.
12:41 Now, if you want to start your digital marketing career in SEO like I did, then I highly recommend
12:46 watching our SEO course for beginners which will teach you how to get free organic traffic
12:50 to your site from start to finish.
12:53 I'll see you there.


---

## Source: how-to-market-your-business-on-social-media.txt

- Original path: `addintional info to add\how-to-market-your-business-on-social-media.txt`
- Size: `14840` bytes
- SHA256: `6615CC9B2D22DE56C9BD9D3ED756792E097DCBAF91A9765D630F19BA1F516710`

---

How To Market Your Business On Social Media

0:00 If you want more eyes on your business and more sales in your bank account social media marketing
0:05 is the way to go. In this video, let's talk about how to market your business on social media.
0:18 Here's the thing, I want to let you in on a big secret. There are three main objectives
0:23 to keep in mind with marketing your business on social media and if you don't have this
0:28 then your strategy is broken. Then I'm going to share with you three proven tactics for landing
0:33 clients, growing your business, getting clients that even you love to work with and that love
0:38 to pay you. What are the objectives of social media marketing? I am so glad you asked, there's three of them.
0:45 You ready for it? Lead generation, lead nurture and lead conversion. If you don't have those
0:51 then your business is broken, and you're just creating content for the sake of content, and
0:55 you're going to keep running on this hamster wheel, and you and your business deserve way
0:58 more than that. Every single piece of content you create to market your business on social media
1:03 should be reaching and accomplishing one of these three or all of these three. Lead generation, lead
1:08 nurture, and lead conversion. Every single piece of content should be reverse engineering what it
1:13 is that you have to sell and how is that piece of content either generating, nurturing or converting.
1:19 Sometimes they can even be leading to all three. For example, I might have a post where I'm telling
1:24 a story about a strategy that I use that changed my life, that changed my business, that helped me
1:29 to make more money and I could say, "Click the link below to get access to this thing. I'm going to
1:34 give it to you for free today." That post, that piece of content, because it has a strong call to action
1:39 is going to generate a lead. Because they can click through that call to action and they can join
1:44 my email list. Which then, they are going to be nurtured. But even if I back up to that
1:49 post itself where I'm telling a really engaging story that I've created it with
1:54 the strategy in mind. That I want to give them this epiphany, that they walk away with thinking,
1:58 "That's the way that I can now also get this result." It's nurturing them before I've even
2:03 generated that lead and then the conversion comes on the back end after they've opted into my email
2:09 list and maybe I have a nurturing email sequence that then brings them towards purchasing. You know,
2:14 a consulting call with me or purchasing one of my courses and that's the conversion. So think about
2:19 how every single piece of content is accomplishing one or all of those three, lead generation,
2:24 lead nurture, and lead conversion. I would recommend you actually write that down on a sticky note,
2:29 put it beside your computer. So that you keep that in mind and never forget that when you're creating
2:32 content. Because we are not here to create content for the sake of content. That's like throwing
2:37 spaghetti at the wall and seeing what sticks. We're not about that. We are doing this intentionally to
2:41 grow our businesses, right? After hearing that I want to know what you think about that?
2:45 Have you ever thought about that before? Have you unintentionally been just creating content
2:50 for the sake of content or have you been using these three? Let me know in the comments below.
2:54 Having an effective social media marketing strategy is so important, why? Because this isn't
2:58 a hobby, this is a business. We're not doing this just for fun although, your business should be fun.
3:04 We want to be creating something that is going to give us an ROI a return on investment. A return on
3:09 energy, a turn on the money that we invest, a return on the time that we invest. Ideally, we want to
3:15 be able to reach more people and you know it's kind of funny when I hear people talk about
3:19 you know get rich quick schemes. I'm not trying to help you get rich slowly either. So when you have a
3:23 strategy that's going to be the fastest path to cash, to growing your business, to reaching more
3:28 people, and making the impact that you want to make, and social media marketing can be one of
3:32 the most effective ways. Because our audiences are on social media all the time and if we know how
3:38 to utilize that to reach them, to give them value, and then to lead gen, lead nurture, and convert them
3:44 into a customer. That's how we're going to grow and that's how we're also going to be able to make our
3:48 impact in the world. By allowing them to grow in the ways that our products and services provide.
3:52 So just posting, just creating content, spray and pray, putting it out, throwing spaghetti at the wall.
3:58 That's not a strategy. Again, go back to lead generation, lead nurture, and lead conversion,
4:04 that is your strategy. So even if you're brand new to business. You might be thinking, "Well, how
4:08 do I create a strategy if I've never done this before? I got to test my content." Absolutely,
4:14 but you're also going to test your content with intention. As a new business or an existing
4:18 business these following tips are going to be really important for you, to make sure that you
4:21 have them in place. First of all, is your branding so that people can recognize you. If I were to say
4:26 to you right now, just do it. What does that make you think of? Nike, right? If I showed you these
4:33 golden arches. What would that make you think of? McDonald's, right? They have these images or they
4:39 have this slogan that is recognizable all over the world. Think about how you can create your branding
4:45 to be memorable for your audience. Something that they resonate with. Something that they get a
4:49 feeling about. I mean, I don't know about you but I think about Nike, and I think about athleticism,
4:53 I think about excellence, I think about hard work and determination, and perseverance. I think about
4:58 McDonald's and I think about happy meals. I loved having meals as a kid. It made me happy. It was
5:03 this like fun experience with my brother, my little brother, or my parents, or my friends. You know, the
5:08 birthday parties and the jungle gyms and stuff. It's a fun experience for me. What do you want
5:12 your business to be about and how can you make sure that everything you put out on social media
5:17 is aligned with that branding? The other piece of creating your branding is going to be the stories
5:21 that you tell. So I recommend for every client that I ever work with or for anyone that ever listens
5:26 to these videos to create a story inventory. Write that down, story inventory. What is your story?
5:34 You have multiple stories. You have your origin story of how you came to be who you are today.
5:39 What was the thing that encouraged you to grow or start this business that you have,
5:44 and what are the pivotal moments along the way that taught you lessons or shaped you into who
5:49 you are today? I would actually recommend having a google doc or a huge notebook where you just brain
5:54 dump all of your stories. This might be childhood lessons, it might be experiences with your family,
6:01 it might be you discovering your expertise or your skills, or what it was that encouraged you to start
6:06 this business that you have today, and anytime you create a piece of content you can go to your story
6:10 inventory and share one of those stories in your content, that people are going to resonate with.
6:15 The reason why I urge you to tell stories in your content is because stories stand the test
6:20 of time. Stories have been brought through from generation to generation. The things that carry
6:25 on our culture or generations of wisdom is the stories that we tell and that we carry on, and as
6:32 you tell these stories people resonate with them. They're captivated, they move in closer,
6:36 they want to hear them. Think about your favorite movies that you love to watch. You're following a
6:41 storyline. You're connecting with the protagonist the main character. You're on the edge of your
6:45 seat watching them as they go through their trials and their tribulations and by the end of the movie
6:50 why do you feel that adrenaline rush or sometimes that like serotonin or dopamine hit? That you just
6:55 love this character that you fall in love with over the last like an hour and a half to two hours.
7:00 Well, it's because you've watched their journey from start to finish. It's that you watch them
7:05 hit rock bottom. You watch them climb their way up and because of that, you cheer louder when they win.
7:11 Whether that means they got the girl or landed the dream job or won the race. You got to see
7:16 what they went through and you resonate with them. Whether you've been in the same experience or that
7:21 you can relate to something similar that you've experienced. When you can share that through your
7:25 content that's going to create this undeniable connection with your audience. You know, I see a
7:29 lot of new businesses focus too much on fun facts and tidbits, instead of going deep into the stories.
7:36 When I first started my business I was a social media manager and I was also a full-time dental
7:40 hygienist. That was one of my first jobs is, I was doing social media for the dental office
7:45 I worked at and sure I could have made posts about how many times a day to brush your teeth and
7:49 and fun facts about cavities and fluoride and stuff. But that's not the stuff that makes
7:54 people stop scrolling. That's not the stuff that captivates them and makes them think about it
7:58 for for days on end. The stuff that makes them stop what they're doing and pay attention is the story.
8:04 So, I would find stories from the people at our office or I would make up a funny story about
8:09 our office goldfish and that's the thing that gets a reaction. So think about that as you're
8:13 creating your content. There's nothing wrong with sharing those fun facts and those tidbits. But can
8:18 you tie it into a story that people will actually stop the scroll and pay attention to? Now before
8:24 I tell you about my proven tactics to landing your dream clients through social media. Make sure that
8:28 you hit that subscribe button. If you're an entrepreneur and you're looking to up-level
8:32 and grow and scale your business, make sure to subscribe. Because I'm putting out videos
8:35 like this every week. Okay, how to land your dream clients on social media? I have three tactics I'm
8:40 going to share with you. The first one is a strong call to action at the end of every single video.
8:44 Remember when I was giving the example about lead generation. Maybe I tell this really great story
8:49 and I segue it into giving them the answer to that if they click the link below and they get the PDF,
8:55 or the ebook or they watch the next video or they sign up for a webinar. Whatever that thing
9:00 is that next step that I want them to take. By telling them what to do, by inviting them, that is
9:06 a call to action. That's me saying, "Here's the next step that you can take to get more value from me.
9:11 Click the link below." It's a strong call to action. When I first started creating content on social
9:16 media I didn't know what that was. I didn't know that I'm supposed to tell them what to
9:19 do next. I think I kind of assumed like, "Well, if they like the video, maybe they'll subscribe or
9:24 maybe they'll reach out to me, maybe they'll email me." You can't assume that because they don't know
9:29 that they're supposed to do that or they don't have a reason to. You have to give them a reason to act.
9:33 So having a strong call to action telling them where to go, that's where you also get to actively
9:38 pursue that relationship. It's like being on a date with somebody, if you were on a date and you wanted
9:43 to have that second date. I mean, sure you can play hard to get but they might also be thinking,
9:48 "Did that even go well? Do they like me? Should this go anywhere?", and then what if that's
9:52 like a misconnection. Instead, just ask for the second date, ask them to take action click the
9:57 link below, join your email list and continue that relationship. Speaking of dating, the next
10:02 concept that I love to share is know, like, trust. Your audience to be able to take that action
10:08 needs to know you, like you, and trust you, and I use this dating analogy to create that image for you.
10:13 That you're not just going right in for the proposal. You're nurturing your audience through
10:18 this valuable content. So that when you do have that call to action it's a no-brainer. Because
10:24 they know you, they like you, they trust you. if you didn't take the effort to date your audience.
10:28 It would be the equivalent of someone coming up to you on the street and being like, "Hey want to get
10:33 married?". You're like, "I don't know you. You don't know my last name. You haven't taken me to dinner."
10:38 You gotta get to know your audience and they need to get to know you, and that's by creating valuable
10:42 content. Now, that's not to say that you can't have calls to action your content right from the get-go.
10:47 But that you lead with value before you bring them to a call to action, and the way we do that
10:53 is through the third tactic that I like to call the 80/20 rule. Now this can be 80% and 20%
10:59 in one piece of content or it can be 80/20 in your whole content structure. So an example is
11:05 you know the post example that I gave you before. Where I told a story and that's like 80 percent of
11:10 the post and 20 percent of the post was the call to action. So I led with value, I told them a story
11:16 that hopefully tugged at their heartstrings or showed them the path and painted that that picture
11:21 of what life could be like if they follow this strategy that I'm about to tell them when they
11:25 click the link below. So having the 80/20 rule is going to be really important for building that
11:29 know like trust factor. Dating your audience and leading to the call to action in a way that isn't
11:34 abrasive like going straight for the proposal. So remember all in all lead with lead generation, lead
11:40 nurture, and lead conversion. When you have that in mind with every piece of content you create
11:44 that's what's going to boost brand loyalty and sales. If you want to learn more about creating
11:49 content that converts into cash flow and builds a raving audience for you. I have a training on just
11:54 the thing. If you click the link below you can get free access to my next training, I'll see you there.


---

## Source: introduction-to-marketing-marketing-101.txt

- SHA256: `00C8FCE68A52E0EC1CA00C833199B67CD44BC378BC81798CE296186ACF5E25B2`

---

Introduction To Marketing | Marketing 101

0:00 If you've ever wanted to know: What marketing is, how it works and how it
0:03 applies to your business, then stick with me, because in this episode I'm going to
0:06 be breaking down exactly what marketing is, how it all works and why it's such an
0:10 important, if not the most important, element to business success. Let's get to
0:15 it. Hey there my name is Adam Earhart,
0:18 marketing strategist and welcome to The Marketing Show where we help you grow your
0:21 business generate more leads, customers, and sales by making way better marketing,
0:24 so if you're interested in learning the latest and greatest marketing strategies,
0:28 tools, tips, tricks and tactics, well, you may want to consider subscribing and
0:31 hitting that notification bell. Alright, let's talk marketing, something I
0:35 love to do. As I fell in love with marketing years ago, when I first got
0:38 interested in figuring out why people buy and act the way they do and later
0:42 how to influence and persuade essentially affect human behavior.
0:45 Because, whether you have a business or not Marketing happens pretty much every
0:49 day. Any time you try to convince friends or family to see a certain movie or eat
0:54 at this restaurant or not that or basically try to convince them to do
0:57 anything, Marketing is happening. Also, marketing is
1:00 really important to understand, because you are constantly being marketed to by
1:03 everyone from everywhere. From something as simple as someone trying to sell you
1:07 a pair of shoes to something a lot bigger, like buying a new house or moving
1:11 across the country or even changing careers. All of this, requires and uses
1:16 marketing. So, with all that said, let's dive into it. But first, let's cover what
1:19 marketing is not, so once we've got that clear, we can cover exactly what it is.
1:23 Now, I know when I first got involved with marketing my understanding was
1:27 pretty limited. I didn't really have a clear picture of exactly what marketing
1:30 was so I also kind of fell prey to the notion that marketing was advertising.
1:35 Well, in reality marketing isn't advertising but advertising is marketing.
1:40 Let me explain. Basically, marketing is a major business function that's made up
1:44 of a bunch of other parts, one of which of course is advertising, but there's
1:48 also a ton others like: PR, market research, social media, content marketing,
1:52 search engine marketing or search engine optimization, pricing and pricing
1:56 psychology, copywriting and one of my personal favorites, direct response or
2:00 direct response marketing and quite a few more. So, saying that marketing is
2:04 just advertising and kind of like saying that finance is just taxes or HR is just
2:09 hiring people or legal is just not getting sued. Sure, these are all
2:12 important but there just one piece of the entire puzzle. This
2:15 is why if you're just getting started in marketing can seem pretty overwhelming
2:19 which is why my suggestion is to find an area that you find interesting,
2:22 fascinating, like say social media or content marketing, start there really get
2:27 some roots and branch out later. Alright, so now that we've got that covered,
2:30 what exactly is marketing? Well, as I've just covered, you know that marketing is
2:34 made up of a bunch of different sub segments things like: content marketing,
2:37 email marketing and social media, all the things we talked about. But what exactly
2:41 is the nature of marketing, which sounds like some kind of documentary "The Nature
2:46 of Marketing" - on this week's episode of The Nature of Marketing - Well, one of the
2:52 first things you learn in any first-year marketing course is the four P´s of
2:55 marketing: product, price, place and promotion. Product being the details
2:59 around whatever product or service is being sold. Price being the price, kind of
3:04 obvious, but there's obviously a lot more that goes into it than just that. Place
3:07 is where the products being sold and promotion, we're just kind of the fun
3:11 stuff. This is essentially how you're gonna sell more of the product and what
3:14 you're gonna do to get the word out about your service and while all of that
3:18 is accurate and true and the four P´s do make up a part of marketing, well, I
3:22 prefer a simpler easier to understand definition. Marketing in its most basic
3:26 sense, is communicating value to your customers. It's essentially answering the
3:30 question of your customers "Why should I care?" Marketing helps people solve their
3:34 problems by clearly defining and delivering solutions and really
3:38 explaining the benefits of the solution so they can get better results. Marketing
3:42 is about connecting with people, understanding their pains and their
3:44 problems and their frustrations, making them feel understood so that you can
3:48 position your business as the solution to their problems and essentially just
3:52 make them feel better and marketing is a really powerful force. As anyone that's
3:56 been in business for any length of time can tell you, it's not always the best
3:59 product or service that wins, in fact it rarely is, rather it's the product or
4:02 service with the best marketing. Like it or not, that's just kind of how it works
4:06 which is why having good marketing is just so important. So my favorite
4:10 definition of marketing is that it's communicating value to your customers
4:13 but there's another side entirely that's rarely talked about but still equally
4:17 powerful and that's creating value for your customers.
4:20 You see, marketing can create value and your marketing in and of itself can be
4:25 valuable. An example could be a blog post that helps someone out,
4:29 even before any money has changed hands or an advertisement someone sees that
4:33 brightens their day and makes them laugh whether they choose to buy or not or the
4:37 way that a product or service is delivered, the packaging let's say which
4:40 is so luxurious and over-the-top that it makes the customer feel special just for
4:45 having purchased it. You see, economics assumes that people make buying
4:48 decisions rationally, logically and with perfect information but this is rarely,
4:53 pretty much never the case. We as humans are emotional, often highly illogical and
4:57 rarely have the full set of facts when making any decision, which explains why
5:01 marketing is so important and so powerful. After all, if we made all of our
5:05 purchasing decisions based solely on logic and utility and the value we would
5:09 get from these products, well, the entire luxury goods market wouldn't even exist
5:14 but not all marketing is created equal. You see, when it comes to marketing like
5:17 when it comes to pretty much anything, there's good marketing and there's bad
5:21 marketing. Bad marketing is all of the reasons that marketing gets such a bad
5:24 reputation. It's that hype, spammy, over-the-top sleazy salesy icky kind of
5:31 marketing that we all know we've all seen and nobody likes. It's the stuff
5:35 that looks cheap, makes people feel dirty, even just looking at it and promotes bad
5:39 products or bad services that really helps nobody. It's the fake countdown
5:43 timers you sometimes see on websites the going-out-of-business sales that never
5:47 end and the pushy promotion of useless products. That's bad marketing. Good
5:51 marketing on the other hand, helps customers achieve their goals, makes them
5:54 feel better about themselves and has the power to truly change the world. So my
5:58 question to you is: What kind of marketer do you want to be? That that was a
6:01 rhetorical question. If you say the bad guy, I got nothing for you but if you say
6:05 the good kind and I know you did, then make sure to check out this video right
6:08 here which I've got linked up on the page which is going to give you even
6:11 more practical and effective marketing strategies to help take your business
6:14 and your marketing to the next level and way beyond that. Alright, so thanks so
6:18 much for watching, make sure to check out that video now and we'll catch you next
6:21 time on The Marketing Show

5:57 you know where is he where is he I'm

[00:10:10] for that company formation session we talk about it in depth well brand does start with your vision although the slightly different thing in the brand world is it's really about what are you changing in the world what is it that you're trying to make an impact with a little bit different than how you're building your company although the two of course are very closely aligned and then the second key part is the promise what do we promise to customers at the most fundamental level so what is if you think about thinking about this

[00:10:34] something that we've covered that that might add to this value proposition and we cover the value proposition in that whole workshop so the value proposition relates very closely to promise and then the piece that gets very interesting and exciting that jameis is going to bring to life is the attributes of your brand what actually makes this a brand that people can get behind and ultimately that's expressed in emotion personality and style so i've glass glass uh sorry glossed over that quite quickly intentionally

[00:11:34] going out effectively to pitch yourself so think about how you the founders your team and your culture and some of the things that we talked about in for the company formation session can be embodied in your brand because it's actually one of the most truly realistic and authentic parts of what becomes a startup's brand and so thinking about that early will help you formulate what it is that people will take away from you and then how you execute is actually what people will measure you on you know you can claim all the things

[00:12:04] you want in the world but if you don't deliver on high value for example nobody's going to attribute that to your brand likewise if you don't deliver on great customer service and you're trying to make that as a promise that's not going to be consistent and we'll talk about how that important that is so to bring this to life i happen to be in the boardroom of a public company of mine demandware and so i'm going to tell you right now because it's public we can't disclose all of this information on the site and we had a real live case study sure

[00:12:32] enough uh we were at the demand web board meeting and we were talking about a company that's now you know an eight year overnight success having gone public this year could evolve its prime its brand what could we do to move the needle now that we've become a substantive company uh with market leadership position to really accelerate the next stage of our brand so with that i'd like to introduce jameis to uh bring it to life and tell us how did he do it thank you michael can everyone hear me okay it's a chronic fear as a marketer that

[00:13:05] you're not heard so it's the kind of thing that i like to check whenever i start talking so thank you for the intro i'm here to talk about a living case study so this is ongoing right now and what i want to do is take you through some of the rationale that we had about our branding and take you through some of the you know analysis that we did and then ultimately show you a couple of instances about how we're starting to execute it um and the execution is not yet live so you guys are getting a sneak peek at a lot of

[00:13:30] this let's bounce this along one more good so um as michael said earlier early stage when you're a smaller organization it's very easy to sort of deal with branding particularly when you're in a marketing organization of just a couple of people because you are the brand and you can make these brand choices along the way and you just sort of know instinctively whether something's what you want to represent or not but as you grow as an organization and as in in our case we got a much larger platform now with a much larger

[00:13:58] microphone it was time for us to look back and really institutionalize the brand you know what are the brand attributes of us that we wanted to build equity around as we execute in lots of different markets and so what we did is we looked back at and did an analysis with our customers and the broader market to get a sense for what are the attributes that we're known for and also what are the challenges that we have in the marketplace and what are the things that we're trying to get done and i'd summarize it simply by saying

[00:14:24] that we're here to try and change the criteria in the buying cycle so demandware in this case as an e-commerce platform company was selling into a market where there was already sort of a software space but we were doing this in a very different way which was to deliver this as an on-demand service so our goal in our marketing and in our branding is to help the market change its mind and change its perspective about how it evaluates us and thinks about us so this is overall for us about how do we build on what we've done and change the change

[00:14:52] the criteria you know for us it was about and i'm not going to go through each one of these things but a sense of both moving from how we had marketed in the past and also how buyers thought about it to this new set of things that we wanted them to consider you know from cost to advantage from managing software to growing the business for mid-sized companies to really the sense of high-growth brands and so our challenge was how do we then sort of move from the left-hand side all the way over to the right and get people

[00:15:22] to think about things differently when we did an analysis of the market what we saw was that everybody for the most part was playing in the blue boxes where everyone was talking about either e-commerce or technology and talking about it as either features or benefits and occasionally we'd see people that were moving to the upper right and the upper the lower right and the upper left but what there is was this great opportunity in the green squares where there was this intersection of what are the rewards that individual buyers want

[00:03] Have you ever wanted to clone a website front end and back end, but every YouTube video you found was a confusing mess? I've been there. I spent days searching for a reliable solution only to find tutorials that told me to copy inspect elements, paste links into chat GPT for code, use complicated tools, or spend forever prompting AI.

[00:22] But don't worry, I found a real way to clone literally any website front and back in just minutes. No coding, no BS, and you'll have it deployed online. All right, let's dive into step one. For this, we'll use a super handy Chrome extension called Save All Resources. The link is in the description below.

[00:41] Once installed, navigate to the website you want to clone. For this tutorial, I've chosen this portfolio site because I wanted to create something just like it. Now right click inspect here. Click on this icon and then resources saver. Click on save all resources. Choose where you want to keep them.

[01:02] It will download all the necessary files HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and even Bootstrap backend files into a neat organized folder on your computer. Now that you have all the raw materials, we will use an AI tool called Trey AI to actually assemble your website from those extracted resources. After installing Trey AI, open it up.

[01:23] Go to files, open folder, and then choose the folder where your downloaded website files are located. Next, click on add agents, select builder, and copy and paste this exact prompt. Trey AI will then think and work on its own. Your only task is to grant it all the necessary access and permissions when prompted.

[01:53] This powerful agent will review your code, fix it in real time, link all your files, and resolve any bugs. If the preview doesn't look exactly like the original website, just prompt again. You can say something like, 'It doesn't look like the original one.' Or ask it to fix a specific section. A little patience here goes a long way.

[02:16] You can even give this builder a screenshot of a particular area if a certain part isn't showing properly. Just click on the images button and select the screenshot. Once you're satisfied with the preview, hitrl + s to save the whole code. You've now built a functional website. Now for the final and arguably easiest part, step three, deploy your app with Netlefi.

[02:38] Head over to netlifi.app. If you don't have an account, it's free and takes literally seconds to sign up. Once logged in, simply drag and drop your website's project folder, the one generated by Trey AI, directly into the Netlefi deployment area. Netlefi automatically detects your files, sets up everything, and within seconds provides you with a unique live URL for your website.

[03:06] To change the URL for your cloned website, go to projects, click on the project you just created, then project configuration, and finally change project name. You can write anything that suits your project best. And just like that, your cloned website is live on the internet. No complex server setups, no complicated configurations.

[03:27] Just drag, drop, and deploy. So there you have it. The ultimate no BS guide to cloning any website front end and back end in just three incredibly simple steps using three amazing tools. It's not just   keywords or locations.

 It's you have   templates and you have variables.   Typically the more val variables you   have the better and these templates are   filled in with the variables and then   you have dependencies based on the   variables.  So this   continues instead of writing posts one   by one you build a template and feed it   data to spin up thousands of pages that   each target a search query. we get over 1 million visitors per month   from Google via programmatic SEO and   this drives over eight figures in   revenue for my business contact app I'll   screens share and I'll show you the   exact process of doing keyword research   scraping content finding data rewriting   content with chat GTP generating Pages   indexing and getting pages to rank and   finally tracking the results welcome to   how to start a startup where I'll go   into not just what to do but how to do   it if you watch this entire video series   email me and I'll be open to investing   up to $50,000 in your business you'd   also get 1 hour of coaching per week   from myself and other mentors that have   built million dooll businesses visit   Athena dovc for more details we're on a   mission to help 1 million Founders start   their first business and we want to   start Silicon Valley ecosystems all   around the world so we can accelerate   science technology and progress let's   get started on how to do programmatic   SEO here are our SEO Pages we have 7   million of these Pages targeting person   name email for example Elon Musk email

  and in total these Pages have generated   over a million in traffic last month   here's another example from canver where   they target templates so you have sales   templates background templates help   templates another example is Airbnb   targeting vacation rentals for different   countries like the United States or   Philippines with thousands of these   pages to make these Pages we start with   keyword research a keyword consists of a   modifier and a key term for example   person name plus email or in airbnb's   case a location plus vacation rent tool   for these keywords first we want to   check does the traffic convert we want   to check the conversion rate to sign up   as well as if the traffic actually buys   your product for example Airbnb does not   advertise on New York hotels because   presumably this keyword doesn't convert   as well for them the next thing to check   is the traffic volume contact out's key   term email gets 37 million searches per   month and canvas key term template gets   17 million searches per month I use HRS   to check traffic volumes because it

  gives a more precise number than Google   keywords tool which you could also use   since it incorporates the Google data   along with clickstream data and other   data sources also Google only gives you   the search volumes for the keyword by   itself email whereas hat trfs will give   you the keyword volume for all other   terms that contain your main keyword   email in my case the next step is to   find modifiers with high traffic for   example in contact out's case we were   targeting person name email so we want   to find people's names that are like   really popular and have a lot of track   so lots of people will search for their   email so what we did was we took a list   of the most common first names and a   list of the most common last names or   like 100,000 names for each category and   then we combined them we would have like   millions of full names and then we ran   this list of full names through PFS and   we were able to get the traffic volumes   for the most searched names you know   James Smith email name plus email what

  we also did was we went onto LinkedIn we   did a scrape of of all of LinkedIn and   we looked at the people with the most   followers for example Bill Gates Richard   Branson Reed Hoffman and we made Pages   for those people because you know they   have a lot of followers something we   also did was look at what pages on   LinkedIn get the most SEO traffic so on   RFS it will show you a list of people on   LinkedIn that get the most traffic you   can also do this for other social   networks like Facebook or Twitter to   find the pages that get the most traffic   which correlate to the most searched for   and the most famous people presumably   people would want to find their emails   we also looked at competitors obviously   to see what keywords our competitors are   targeting for example our competitor   rocket reach is also targeting you know   Elon Musk email an obvious place to   start is just to look at the terms on   RFS that contain email and you know

  there's 1.2 million keywords here   however the reason we went beyond this   was most of these terms are not the most   relevant you know Yahoo email email   signature is not something we want to   Target and whil there's 1.2 million it's   quite hard to like sort through these so   yeah start here but you can go much   Beyond this and then another question   that you guys might think of is what   about the keyword difficulty what about   how hard it is to rank which I think is   important if you're doing a programmatic   seur site and you're targeting like I   don't know maybe a few thousand or a few   hundred keywords then it will matter   however with contact out we're targeting   millions of keywords like we have you   know 2.6 million or like seven million   pages that we're targeting so really   keyword difficulty doesn't matter that   much we're going for like volume and   breadth of keywords and we're just kind   of doing a scattershot approach of be   like okay let's have millions of   keywords and hopefully some of them will   rank any site with millions of pages is   not going to rank for high competition

  keywords anyway for example the main   keyword we target like email finder all   the pages on the first page they only   have a few thousand pages in total our   competitors that have millions of pages   like you know rocket reach Lucia AP   poloo none of them are ranking on this   page even though they are actually the   most popular tools because they have   millions of pages you know rocket reach   has to buy ads because you know they   don't have much Authority on any   individual   page yeah so that's why I guess contact   outs case is unique that we don't look   at like competition as much like keyword   difficulty but for other sides you   probably would need to look at this   quite a lot so if we were to generalize   this method there's two approaches first   is to just list out a bunch of popular   keywords I come up with like a list of   million names and then search it on hats   and the second one is to look at okay   what keywords are other sites other   directory sites with like millions of   pages ranking for here's some other   large directory sites that you can check

  for traffic levels on hats to get a list   of popular business names it would be   crunch base where they will give you a   list of or businesses that you can check   or White Pages if you're looking for a   list of professions there's the job   sites like indeed and as we already   covered for people there are the social   networks like LinkedIn Facebook Etc that   you can see one of the most trafficked   Pages now onto talking about generating   the pages the first step of this is to   get the data like we need data to   generate millions of these pages and   there are scraping companies like right   data like core signal Z is another one   where they have pre-s scraped data sets   for example you can buy an entire scrape   of LinkedIn or crunch Bas glass store   yel a lot of these popular online   directories the price depends on the   data set everything from a few thousand   to upwards of 100 Grand or or six   figures depending on what you want so

  what we did was we bought some of these   data sets for LinkedIn data as well as   crunch Bas data so you know people   profiles company profiles and we use   that to create our Pages for Bill Gates   and everyone else that we have some of   the data sources that you can consider   are kagle where they have a open library   of thousands upon thousands of data sets   everything from anime to sensus data to   Airbnb you know you can use like the who   is records you can actually download all   this from who is API and there's 1.7   billion records which is like who owns   domains and you know what's the contact   data for these owners there's the flight   data which Powers sites like Elon jet   where you can see where every flight is   and you can make a programmatic your   site for tracking where celebrities are   based on their Jets and perhaps get sued   by Elon there's like legal data sets   where all the court records are public   and you can download this and look at it   I don't know maybe you want to create

  some sort of like legal ruling directory   or or something like that medical data   sets same thing you can find like   scientific research papers make some   interesting analysis or websites on that   but records as well are public in the   USA so I don't know like how people   voted so by the way like these company   zit core signal Etc they'll scrape   anything that you want so if you want a   custom scraping project you just go up   to them and be like hey can you scrape   the site and they'll give you a quote   you can do it yourself as well I mean   you could use like guys on an upw to do   it but you know I found it more time   effective to just use a company and   they'll do it for you so here's   something interesting that a friend of   mine did with podcast scrapes so you   know they'll scrape all the podcast   episodes and transcripts and then he   built a search engine for podcasts that   differentiates Itself by showing you the   most listen to episode for every podcast   so for example gear is the most listen   to episode for J shed's podcast and you   know like alel would have thousands of

  these Pages ranking in Google so another   interesting source of content is the   transcripts in YouTube that you can   scrape through some of these like Chrome   extension tools it's in the HTML   document so you can just look at it why   this is interesting is because you can   transform this data and make it unique   or or into an article so you take the   YouTube transcript from a video you put   it into chat GTP and you ask it to write   a article on the same topic based on the   points that were brought up in the video   and that's like great free content that   you can generate in a large scale   programmatic way you'd have to like   human review it first and you know make   sure it's good and adds value to the   user but that's another like large   source of data for programmatic pages on   basically any topic you want yeah so as   you can see this is what I did here I   was like hey take this podcast   transcript that YouTube works as well   and uh write an article about like   bootstrapping and sustainable growth and   as you see here it's pretty decent needs   some editing but you know we can do this   for like basically any topic so we want   to make content that's actually

  generates value so the content needs to   be unique otherwise Google won't rank   our content if it's just a duplicate and   if our content doesn't provide any value   then even if it does rank users will   bounce and go back to Google and then   Google would drop our rankings so   there's no way to get around like   actually generating value for the user   so as I mentioned what we did was we   took the scrape of the LinkedIn profile   we put it over to chat GTP and we're   like write an article 4 Bill Gates based   on his LinkedIn profile and highlight   like the most interesting points here's   some of the content that we were able to   generate based on that also what we did   for Joseph here was we analyzed his   LinkedIn profile for certain personality   keywords and we were able to produce a   personality graph this is how like   creative this person is on a scale of 1   to 10 or here's how well they   communicate uh Etc you can estimate   salary ranges based on a person's job   title or their location or the company

  they work at so here's some analysis we   did there and and displayed on our   directory Pages as well Zoom info a   competitor of ours creates org chart   data based off of the LinkedIn data so   they would go and scrap companies on   LinkedIn where it will show show you the   different roles on LinkedIn and then   they would do some analysis to say like   okay who reports to who based on their   job title and and roll on LinkedIn   another interesting thing is Tech   install data so telling you what tech   stack a particular company users and   we're able to get this from analyzing   the job posts of a particular company if   you look at a company's open positions a   lot of the times they will talk about   the technologies that you need to know   to get that job in this case you need to   know like the Windows Server DNS dfp GP   I don't know what these are anyway these   are the technologies that this company   uses and from this Zoom info was able to

  you know create this Tech stack Tech   install data that you know a particular   company uses yeah so those are all the   ways that you can create unique content   and generate value for the user Beyond   just scraping some content and   republishing it which weren't really do   much for you moving on to talking about   indexing our content because if we don't   index our Pages then we'll never get   traffic and we'll never rank in Google   indexing is the first part so simply for   contact out we submitted our Pages maybe   half a million or a million at a time to   Google search console and uh so far we   have about 7.8 million Pages index   that's like growing every day the things   that we found to impact your indexing   rate is your domain Authority so this is   the age of your domain like how high   quality is your site you know there's a   lot of like user stats so maybe you know   from the Google can get from chrome   about like how users engage with your   site what the retention is the links to   your site is probably the main one which   determines like hey how authoritative is

  it also the number of pages you   currently have index we have quite a lot   nearly 8 million Pages index so to get   more pages index is not too easy for us   generally we find that when you have a   lot of pages yeah it gets harder keyword   selection was a big one for us so what   we did initially was we released maybe a   million of these Pages like these uh you   know Bill Gates email person name email   pages and we were like wow we got all   this traffic these pages are indexing   let's like blow this up so we decided to   release like a 100 million of these   pages and uh what do you know like none   of the pages index Google is like hey um   I don't want to index 100 pages so what   we found to help was when we selected   names or keywords that had traffic   volume for example like the names of   like famous people or the names of like   popular businesses or it might be like   you know email pattern for mckenzie.com   or management emails for McKenzie when   we selected the popular keywords it were   getting a lot of traffic our indexing

  rate was much better as well whereas if   we just release random Pages most of   those probably would never search for in   terms of like you know random names or I   don't know like every name of every   person on LinkedIn and hence like Google   doesn't want to index them the other   things we were doing for indexing is   obviously creating a Sit map that links   to every page because you know Link's   primary driver of indexing so from our   homepage it goes through this directory   of of people then there's a link to like   every individual page and then also on   the people Pages they link to each other   so it's like hey these are some related   people that you might also be interested   in uh on the right hand side there's a   lot of factors that determine indexing   here's like a list back links there's   like visits by people in Google Chrome   links in Gmail Google ad campaigns I   don't know how many of these like   actually real is just from a random   Google search there could be some   creative ways that you could game this   we haven't really tried being too   creative here we've just done you know   like links and sitemap submission but   thinking about it and again this is

  for a review in response link exchanges   is something else that you can do there   are some people that say Hey if you swap   links like maybe uh Google has some way   to detect that you can do three-way   exchanges where maybe you know you   promote one site and then another site   promotes you so it's not like a direct   Exchange I think HRA says it doesn't   really matter too much even if it is   like a direct exchange I mean people   like cross-promote all the time it's   just part of business you know you   promote like related products and uh   other people in your industry will   promote like your service I don't see   why uh that that's an issue there the   final one that we use was searching for   brand mentions so people that have   blogged about contact out or have talked   about our Chrome extension but never   linked to our website so we would find   you know just search contact out in   Google and find all the places that   mention up BR and then reach out to   those web Masters and ask for a link   right so how we scaled this up was we   hired a team in the Philippines but you

  just ask everyone in our team when we   interview them is you know how many   books did you read and what did you   learn from them and what's the most   impressive thing that you've   accomplished just gives a good   background to how people think and you   know what what have they leared and   insight into their writing which is   reflective of you know the clarity of   their thinking as well this is our link   building Tracker Where the team will   just put the links that we built what   the domain Authority is when they were   built Etc here's some example   documentation about our process that we   give to our team to do link building so   this is for brand mentions where we   search for people that have mentioned   contact out on the site then you know we   filter the domain authority of these   here's a reference to like a guide by   HFS and here's the email script that we   send out and it's just saying hey like   you mentioned us would you care to like   just link to our website as well that'   be like greatly appreciated that's it   you know follow up a couple times it's   really not like too complicated the   strategy it's more like just consistency   and sting out like a lot of emails I

  think we probably built like 800 or so   links in in total over the course of the   years here's a another like example   Outreach hey Demetri you know you've   mentioned these other tools like   clearbit and sell hack on your article   here's a bit about contact out would be   great to like hear your thoughts on us   and like if you check us out and uh you   know give us like a review or consider   like adding us to to the article then I   think Demetri is like hey do you have   budget for advertising and we're like no   um you know I guess Google doesn't like   people buying links and then he's like   hey uh you know what if I promote you on   like my blog and in return you can you   know promote us on like your blog and   we're like yeah sure that kind of works   one thing to note is that when you're   doing this cross promotion stuff you   want to check that it is a legitimate   site and it's not like spammy and you   know it's not like a link Network or   private blog Network which just a Spam   site where but the only purpose is for   links really easy way to check this is   does this website have traffic if   they're getting traffic then typically   it's a real s and also you know you can

  look at the site like does it look   spammy are they all these like spam   links Etc this one looked pretty good so   we're like sure let's do some cross   promotion here finally there's   monitoring results this is our Google   analytics traffic every month and we can   look at the pages that get traffic from   our programmatic SEO Pages as well as   the pages that don't get traffic for   examp example we found that maybe 70% or   more of the pages that we released don't   actually get any traffic so after a   while we can consider hey do we still   want these Pages live or we can do a   export for every page that receives   traffic from Google analytics we could   also look at our server logs which is   more accurate than analytics yeah so   every page that doesn't get traffic we   can de-index those pages or redirect   those pages to new pages that will   release that could potentially get more   traffic and this is to conserve indexing   budget because as I mentioned the more

The problem with programmatic SEO is   that a lot of people who try to do it   end up generating tons of useless, not   valuable pages that bloats their site.   And by tons, I mean thousands, bloats   their site, wastes their crawl budget,   and just ultimately ruins their SEO,   ruins all of their SEO.

 But I came   across this thread on X a few weeks ago   about Peter Level's programmatic SEO.   Peter Levelvels invented basically   invented indie hacking and the digital   nomad movement. I'm a huge fan of Peter   Levels and this thread broke down his   programmatic SEO really well. Peter   Levels even commented on it and said   this is exactly correct.

 This this   thread breaking down my programmatic SEO   is correct. And I'm going to share his   response after I share the thread. But   the difference is for Peter Level's   programmatic SEO, his pages are useful.   And I'm going to give I mean the thread   gives tons of examples and by the end of   it you'll have a pretty good   understanding of what programmatic SEO   when done properly looks like.

 So this   is from mephreb on X and the link to   this thread will be shared in the   description for this episode. It starts   if you think that Peter Levels is making   money from his ex audience you're so   wrong. It's programmatic SEO and his   execution is insane. And then four of   his businesses are shared remote. Okay.

  590,000 pages. Photo AI 61,000 pages.   Nomad List 20,000 pages. Interior AI   4,000 pages. What's programmatic SEO?   Programmatic SEO is about creating   thousands of high-value pages   automatically, each targeting a specific   keyword or location, so you rank for   everything people search. It's not just   keywords or locations.

 It's you have   templates and you have variables.   Typically the more val variables you   have the better and these templates are   filled in with the variables and then   you have dependencies based on the   variables. So things within your   template change based on the variables   and a lot of people now are adding in AI   as well based on the variables.

 So this   continues instead of writing posts one   by one you build a template and feed it   data to spin up thousands of pages that   each target a search query. That's how   Peter floods Google with 590,000   plus pages. And by the way, you want to   start if you're if you're trying   programmatic SEO, you don't just want to   put up 600,000 pages at once.

 You want   to go slow. You want to scale up slowly.   The thread continues. So, let's start   with remote. Okay. Remote. Okay. Has the   most indexed pages and the most traffic   of all Peter's products. You might think   it's just pages like remote tech stack   jobs, remote jobs in location, remote   tech stack jobs in location, but that's   only the tip of the iceberg.

 Peter built   a unique page for every company that   ever posted a job. Search remote jobs at   company name and chances are you'll see   remote, okay, on page one. And it   doesn't stop there. Every job seeker   also gets a profile page indexed by   Google. And I love this next part. It's   a win-win strategy.

 Recruiters find   talent. Workers get visibility. Remote,   okay, pockets the traffic and shows   Google the site is valuable. I'm going   to read this again. Recruiters find   talent. Workers get visibility. Remote,   okay, pockets the traffic and shows   Google the site is valuable. These pages   aren't pages with meaningless data   that's not going to help visitors.

 These   programmatic SEO pages are actually   useful. The pages are useful for the   intent of the searcher. It's that's   hugely important. And so many people   overlook that. I see that so often. If   you are doing any programmatic SEO   strategy, before you even do it, ask   yourself, are these pages going to be   useful and unique for searchers? So this   thread continues.

 Now imagine hundreds   of these niche pages, each pulling 50 to   100 searches a month. Individually   small, but together that's how remote   pulls around 21,000 Google visitors   every month. Now, let's check Photo AI.   Pulling in about $148,000   per month. 61,000 pages indexed. Ranked   for 7.8,000 keywords, 13.8,000 organic   traffic every month just from Google.

  Peter uses the same programmatic SEO   play. Take every scrap of data, generate   a page, let Google surface it to the   right user. Every category you can dream   of from anime characters to AI footpix   has its own landing page ready to   capture traffic and convert. And it   doesn't stop there. Photo AI also   creates idea inspiration pages for every   photo concept you might search for.

 You   might think that's it, but even user   generated images get their own page,   likely from free plan prompts, but all   get indexed. So trust me when I say he   squeezes every bit of structured and   unstructured data into new pages,   feeding Google an endless supply of   content. You know, I think it's also   important to note this thread at the   top.

 It claims he's not making money   from his ex audience. He is using his ex   audience to launch these products. When   he launches products, they get back   links. They get traffic. They get   branded searches. All of these signal to   search engines that the websites are   important, the the these projects are   important, and because they get more   domain authority and more engagement, it   makes more of their pages rank.

 So, if   you're trying to do programmatic SEO and   you have absolutely no engagement, no   links, start by getting engagement and   links first. Because if you want to put   up tons and tons of pages, many pages,   you're going to need a good amount of   engagement and links for that. and   branded searches.

 Branded search   searches as well. Thread continues. If   you've been following along, you can   already guess what's happening with   Nomad List, too. Every city and country   gets its own page. Looking for costs of   living, internet speed, weather. There's   a page for each same blueprint. Convert   every data point into a standalone page.

  Index it and let Google serve it to the   people searching. This simple play   drives around 8,000 Google visits a   month. traffic worth about $4.9,000   in ads. If you have a   database or a user content, you can do   it, too.

That comes back down to what   we were talking about at the beginning.   If you're putting up thin value, low   value programmatic SEO pages and lots of   them, that's going to hurt your website.   That's going to hurt your brand. It is   not worth it. If you're putting up tons   of pages that are not unique, that is   also going to hurt your brand. But if   you are putting up many unique, valuable   pages with programmatic SEO, it is   perfectly fine. And these pages are   going to be useful for searchers. So, if you can take the info from   YouTube videos be that they that are   yours or not, and post it to your site,   you can also be mentioned. Again, if   this is a a long video, I just I just   compressed all the info on here in like   10 seconds.

 Go watch it if you want to   learn a bit more about how this   automation works, why it's available,   and why you should uh in my opinion   follow this uh I guess bonus tip for   fully automating your SEO workflows.  You can go here and you can   generate newsbased content. And how this   differs basically is that a news-based   content is um we cite it right we when   when you're writing a news piece right   you have to know where you got the info   from. So if I go here generations just   looking at one that I did in the past   right here when you write a piece of   news content right it says it sites the   sources down here.

 So it's different   right? There's different types of   content you can generate with Arvo. Be   the classic SEO article, the news   article, the YouTube blog post, or again   the custom uh templates you can generate   uh you can build out with for your   programmatic SEO. You can do the whole   thing right here. And if you're   wondering if this works, again, if you   go here to the case studies tab, you'll   see that these guys, I think this is   right here, they grew from uh yeah, 0 to   10K month in 24 hours just off of doing   newsbased content. So again,   these are two super quickfire bonus uh   programmatic SEO tips sort of um which   you can uh do right now. And uh by the   way, it's sort of programmatic SEO, just   not in a classic way because you're   leveraging AI. It's basically evolved.   So hopefully this rapidfire course on   programmatic SEO taught you like how and   why things work. Taught you that before   we only use the variables and the   offline, but now we because AI   technology, we get to use the generation   prompt. And the best tool for that,   again, I'm fully biased.   It's fantastic. Give it a go. And as I   promised, if you watch all the way   through, you got a secret coupon code.   So, if you use the code secret secret   10, you get an instant 10% off.
